message.content:
Changelog for Feb 25, 2026

  1) slack search is now first-class

slack is fully integrated across connect → index → search. You can connect workspaces in Integrations, choose channels to index, search/read/grep Slack content, and manage reconnect/ cancel/delete flows more reliably.

  2) faster, safer search flows

improved search responsiveness with request cancellation, timeout fallbacks, and better payload validation. 

  3) home + onboarding overhaul

new Home experience and navigation defaults, better onboarding progression/resume behavior, improved org setup, and a cleaner agent-skill setup path with API key generation/copy UX improvements.

  4) activity + observability upgrades

activity feed now includes richer response snippets, source info, clearer query-type labels/icons, and Oracle job events.


---

A lot of time has passed since this update so I wanted to use GH to check every single commit, PR, and push made from that date until today, which is March 7th, I think. Just check everything but a couple of things that we added, for example:
- spreadsheet indexing so you can index spreadsheets
- another thing is that tracer now delegates tasks to sub-agents so it performs better compared to having one main agent take ur time

content.thinking: The user wants me to check all GitHub activity (commits, PRs, pushes) from February 25, 2026 to March 7, 2026 to compile a changelog. They've already mentioned two known additions (spreadsheet indexing and tracer sub-agents). Let me gather all the Git history.

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive view. Let me organize this into a changelog. The user already mentioned:
1. Spreadsheet indexing
2. Tracer sub-agents

Let me categorize all the changes from Feb 25 to Mar 7.

content.text:
Here's the full changelog from **Feb 25 – Mar 7, 2026**:

---

## Changelog (Feb 25 – Mar 7, 2026)

### New Features

**1) Spreadsheet indexing** (PR #331)
- Upload and index CSV, TSV, XLSX, and XLS files via a new "Spreadsheet" source option
- Header-aware row chunking with dedicated `csv_extractor` pipeline

**2) Tracer sub-agents** (PR #343 branch, commits on Mar 7)
- Tracer now delegates tasks to specialized sub-agents for better performance
- SDK upgrade, structured output fixes, and updated report UI

**3) Tracer fast/deep modes** (PR #314)
- Users can choose between `fast` (Haiku) and `deep` (Opus) modes in the Tracer playground, API, and MCP servers

**4) OpenAPI/Swagger auto-detection** (PR #328)
- Automatically detects and indexes OpenAPI specs from API doc renderer pages (Redoc, Swagger UI, RapiDoc, Stoplight, Scalar)

**5) Papers playground upgrade** (PR #327)
- Direct PDF upload/indexing in the papers playground
- Searchable command-based selector replacing the plain dropdown

**6) Agent auth (headless/API-first)** (PR #324)
- Agents can now obtain `nk_` API keys without a browser
- Headless auth support in the local-sync CLI and new bootstrap token flow

**7) Credit pack invoice display** (PR #312)
- Credit pack purchases now appear in billing invoice history (personal + org)
- Deduplication logic against Stripe invoices

**8) Free plan credit access** (PR #329)
- Free plan users can now use Deep Research, Oracle, and Tracer with personal credits (previously org-gated)

**9) Subscription tier mapping** (commit `da71eb67`, Mar 6)
- New tier mapping service with comprehensive tests

### Embedding Migration

**10) ZeroEntropy embedding migration** (PR #332)
- Fully replaced OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` (1536d) with ZeroEntropy `zembed-1` (2560d)
- New `ZeroEntropyEmbeddingClient` service
- Followup fixes for retrieval regressions (PR #337) and cache/index migration (PR #335)

### UI/UX Improvements

**11) UI overhaul** (PR #319)
- Streamlined navigation across research, search, papers, and datasets
- Refreshed layouts and smoother page transitions

**12) Home page & routing** (PR #339)
- Home renders directly at `/` instead of redirecting to `/home` — removes flicker

**13) Source card fixes** (PR #330)
- Web source cards now show the full URL instead of just the hostname
- Indexing progress persists to DB

**14) Source renaming** (PR #340)
- Path-safe data source rename API route for URL-like identifiers

**15) Source URL validation** (PR #336)
- Rejects non-http(s) URLs in overview modal, Next.js schemas, and FastAPI models

**16) CLI auth resilience** (PR #338)
- 8s slow-loading timeout with refresh button
- Retry with exponential backoff, YC deal propagation to orgs, simplified sidebar

### Oracle Agent Fixes

**17) Oracle prod stability** (PRs #304, #305, #306, #325, #335, #341, #342)
- Fixed `web_fetch` feedback loop wasting 8 iterations
- Handle empty text / `max_tokens` truncation gracefully
- Proper `tool_result` before retrying on `tool_use` blocks
- Fix planner streaming (single JSON action contract, fail runs without real evidence)
- Fix search tool calling issue
- Proactive source discovery for users with many sources (PR #313)
- Source passing as structured fields instead of prompt text (PR #325)

### Performance & Infrastructure

**18) DB query optimization** (PRs #315, #318)
- Fixed index scanning 5,810 keys per page load (43ms → optimized)
- Project lists, data source resolution, and global source browsing all faster

**19) Dashboard load time** (PRs #310, #311)
- Auth-aware rendering to reduce flicker
- Non-blocking onboarding checks

**20) MCP server fixes** (PRs #303, #307)
- Fixed 429s from Cloud Run self-calls (now uses `localhost:PORT` internally)
- Fixed grep timeouts
- Read streaming response body before error handling

**21) Explore chat Bedrock fix** (PRs #333, #334)
- Switched to Anthropic API directly for explore chat to avoid Bedrock Converse API incompatibility
- Configurable Bedrock model ID

**22) Cache invalidation fix** (commit `d1f6d200`, Mar 6)
- Dynamic `source_type` mapping for correct cache invalidation across all source types

### Billing & Limits

**23) Activity page fix** (PR #308)
- Total queries no longer caps at 100; uses proper stats count
- Filter bar overflow fixed

**24) Credit refund fixes** (PR #295 on Feb 22, plus commits Mar 4)
- Refund now targets correct pool when purchased credits were used
- Oracle/Deep Research refund on timeout/failure
- Personal credit fallback made universal

### Other Fixes

- **Prod console errors** (PR #320): Clerk deprecated prop, Recharts sizing, null trim
- **API key preview** (PR #309): Removed non-functional reveal/copy buttons
- **Error logging noise** (PR #299): HTTPException guards, rate limit conflict severity
- **Circular dependency** (PR #298): Removed `"nia": "."` self-dependency

---

That covers **42 merged PRs** (#303–#342) from Feb 25 through Mar 7. The biggest themes beyond spreadsheet indexing and tracer sub-agents are the **ZeroEntropy embedding migration**, **Oracle agent stabilization**, and **DB/performance optimizations**.

     4→Welcome to the Nia AI documentation repository! This repository contains the official documentation for Nia AI, powered by [Mintlify](https://mintlify.com/).
     5→

     8→</Note>

    11→

    79→

   103→```

   110→

1→--- 2→title: 'Welcome to Nia by Nozomio Labs' 3→description: 'API for technical, up-to-date context' 4→--- 5→ 6→ 7→ 8→ 9→ 10→## What is Nia? 11→ 12→Nia is an API layer that provides agents with up-to-date, continuously monitored context from libraries, research papers, and technical documentation, saving you hours of manual ingestion. 13→ 14→It can also be used with coding agents like Cursor or Claude as an MCP server, giving them reliable external context so they don't hallucinate. 15→ 16→--- 17→ 18→## Get Started 19→ 20→ 24→ ```bash 25→ npx nia-wizard@latest 26→ ``` 27→ 28→ 29→ 30→ 35→ Detailed setup for all IDEs and manual configuration 36→ 37→ 38→ 43→ Explore Nia's search, indexing, and research capabilities 44→ 45→ 46→ 51→ Sync local folders, databases, and chat history to Nia 52→ 53→ 54→ 59→ Deep autonomous research across codebases and documentation 60→ 61→ 62→ 67→ Learn how to use Nia effectively with real examples 68→ 69→ 70→ 71→--- 72→ 73→## Quick Links 74→ 75→ 76→ 81→ Complete API documentation 82→ 83→ 84→ 89→ Common issues and solutions 90→ 91→ 92→ 97→ Join our Discord community 98→ 99→ 100→ 101→

1→---
     2→title: 'Agent Tools'
     3→description: 'Comprehensive guide to all Nia MCP tools and capabilities.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→<Steps>
     7→  <Step title="Search Package Source Code (No indexing required!)">
     8→    Ask your coding agent: *"Use package search to find how error handling is implemented in the `requests` Python library"* or *"Search the numpy package for array manipulation examples"*
     9→  </Step>
    10→
    11→  <Step title="Index Documentation, Repositories, Research Papers, or Datasets">
    12→    Try indexing public documentation, a repository, research papers, or HuggingFace datasets:
    13→    - *"Index https://docs.trynia.ai/"*
    14→    - *"Index https://github.com/browser-use/browser-use"*
    15→    - *"Index https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345"* (arXiv papers)
    16→    - *"Index https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k"* (HuggingFace datasets)
    17→    - *"Use nia deep research to compare the best GraphRAG frameworks and then index the one with least latency."*
    18→  </Step>
    19→
    20→  <Step title="Monitor Progress and Explore">
    21→    Check your indexed resources using `manage_resource`:
    22→    - *"List my resources"* or *"Check the status of my indexing jobs"*
    23→    - Visit [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai) to see all your indexed content
    24→  </Step>
    25→</Steps>
    26→
    27→## Available Tools
    28→
    29→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    30→  <Card title="Universal Indexing" icon="database">
    31→    - **`index`** - Index repos, docs, research papers, or local folders (auto-detects type)
    32→    - **`auto_subscribe_dependencies`** - Index all dependencies from package.json, requirements.txt, and more
    33→  </Card>
    34→
    35→  <Card title="Universal Search" icon="magnifying-glass">
    36→    - **`search`** - Semantic search across repos, docs, papers, and local folders
    37→    - **`nia_package_search_hybrid`** - AI-powered semantic search in package source code
    38→  </Card>
    39→
    40→  <Card title="Navigation & Reading" icon="book">
    41→    - **`nia_read`** - Read content from repo, docs, package, or local folder
    42→    - **`nia_grep`** - Regex search in repo, docs, package, or local folder
    43→    - **`nia_explore`** - Browse file structure (tree/ls actions)
    44→  </Card>
    45→
    46→  <Card title="Resource Management" icon="folder">
    47→    - **`manage_resource`** - List, status, rename, delete, or subscribe to resources
    48→  </Card>
    49→
    50→  <Card title="Research & Analysis" icon="flask">
    51→    - **`nia_research`** - AI research with 3 modes: quick, deep, oracle
    52→    - **`nia_advisor`** - Analyze your code against documentation for recommendations
    53→  </Card>
    54→
    55→  <Card title="Context Sharing" icon="comments">
    56→    - **`context`** - Cross-agent context sharing with memory types (scratchpad, episodic, fact, procedural)
    57→  </Card>
    58→</CardGroup>
    59→
    60→## Complete Tool Reference
    61→
    62→<AccordionGroup>
    63→  <Accordion title="Package Search (No Indexing Required)">
    64→    ### `nia_package_search_hybrid`
    65→    Semantic search in package source code with optional regex filtering. Search 3,000+ packages from PyPI, NPM, Crates.io, and Go modules without indexing.
    66→
    67→    **Parameters**:
    68→    - `registry` (str): One of "npm", "py_pi", "crates_io", "golang_proxy"
    69→    - `package_name` (str): Package name (for Go: "org/repo" format)
    70→    - `query` (str): Natural language search query
    71→    - `pattern` (str, optional): Regex filter to narrow results
    72→    - `version` (str, optional): Specific package version
    73→
    74→    **Example**:
    75→    ```
    76→    # Ask your agent:
    77→    "Search the requests package for how error handling works"
    78→    "Find authentication examples in the fastapi package"
    79→    ```
    80→
    81→    **Best for**: Understanding implementation patterns, finding usage examples, exploring package architecture.
    82→  </Accordion>
    83→
    84→  <Accordion title="Universal Indexing Tools">
    85→    ### `index`
    86→    Universal indexing tool that intelligently detects and indexes GitHub repositories, documentation, research papers, or local folders.
    87→
    88→    **Auto-Detection**:
    89→    - GitHub URLs (containing "github.com") → Repository indexing
    90→    - arXiv URLs (containing "arxiv.org") → Research paper indexing
    91→    - HuggingFace dataset URLs (containing "huggingface.co/datasets") → Dataset indexing
    92→    - Local folder path provided → Local folder indexing
    93→    - All other URLs → Documentation indexing
    94→
    95→    **Parameters**:
    96→    - `url` (str, optional): GitHub repository URL, documentation site URL, arXiv ID/URL, or HuggingFace dataset URL (not needed for local folders)
    97→    - `resource_type` (str, optional): Override auto-detection - "repository", "documentation", "research_paper", "huggingface_dataset", or "local_folder"
    98→
    99→    **Repository-specific**:
   100→    - `branch` (str, optional): Branch to index (defaults to main branch)
   101→
   102→    **Documentation-specific**:
   103→    - `url_patterns` (List[str], optional): URL patterns to include in crawling
   104→    - `exclude_patterns` (List[str], optional): URL patterns to exclude from crawling
   105→    - `only_main_content` (bool, default=True): Extract main content only, skip nav/footer
   106→    - `check_llms_txt` (bool, default=True): Check for /llms.txt file
   107→    - `llms_txt_strategy` (str, default="prefer"): Strategy for handling llms.txt - "prefer", "only", or "ignore"
   108→    - `wait_for` (int, optional): Wait time in milliseconds for dynamic content to load
   109→    - `include_screenshot` (bool, optional): Capture screenshots of pages
   110→    - `max_age` (int, optional): Cache max age in seconds
   111→
   112→    **Local folder-specific**:
   113→    - `folder_path` (str, optional): Absolute path to a folder on your machine (e.g., `/Users/me/project`)
   114→    - `folder_name` (str, optional): Display name for the folder (defaults to folder basename)
   115→    - `files` (List[dict], optional): List of `{path, content}` dicts (auto-read if `folder_path` provided)
   116→
   117→    **Usage**: Monitor progress with `manage_resource` action "status"
   118→
   119→    **Examples**:
   120→    ```
   121→    # Index a repository (auto-detected)
   122→    index("https://github.com/owner/repo", branch="main")
   123→
   124→    # Index documentation (auto-detected)
   125→    index("https://docs.example.com", url_patterns=["*/api/*"])
   126→
   127→    # Index a research paper from arXiv
   128→    index("https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345")
   129→
   130→    # Index a HuggingFace dataset (auto-detected)
   131→    index("https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k")
   132→
   133→    # Index a local folder
   134→    index(folder_path="/Users/me/notes", folder_name="My Notes")
   135→    ```
   136→
   137→    ### `get_github_file_tree`
   138→    Get file and folder structure directly from GitHub API without indexing.
   139→
   140→    **Parameters**:
   141→    - `repository` (str, required): Repository identifier (owner/repo format)
   142→    - `branch` (str, optional): Branch name (defaults to repository's default branch)
   143→    - `include_paths` (List[str], optional): Only show files in these paths
   144→    - `exclude_paths` (List[str], optional): Hide files in these paths
   145→    - `file_extensions` (List[str], optional): Only show these file types
   146→    - `exclude_extensions` (List[str], optional): Hide these file types
   147→    - `show_full_paths` (bool, default=False): Show full paths instead of tree structure
   148→
   149→    **Best for**: Quick repository exploration without indexing overhead.
   150→
   151→    ### `auto_subscribe_dependencies`
   152→    Automatically index all dependencies from your project's manifest file. Parses the manifest, maps packages to documentation URLs, and subscribes to indexed docs.
   153→
   154→    <Frame>
   155→      <iframe
   156→        src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/qFQDsElWyO0"
   157→        title="Dependency Auto-Subscribe Demo"
   158→        className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
   159→        allowFullScreen
   160→      />
   161→    </Frame>
   162→
   163→    **Supported Manifests**:
   164→    - `package.json` (npm/Node.js)
   165→    - `requirements.txt` (Python)
   166→    - `pyproject.toml` (Python)
   167→    - `Cargo.toml` (Rust)
   168→    - `go.mod` (Go)
   169→    - `Gemfile` (Ruby)
   170→
   171→    **Parameters**:
   172→    - `manifest_content` (str, required): Raw content of the manifest file
   173→    - `manifest_type` (str, optional): Auto-detected from content if not provided
   174→    - `include_dev_dependencies` (bool, default=False): Include devDependencies
   175→    - `max_new_indexes` (int, default=150): Maximum new documentation indexes to start
   176→    - `dry_run` (bool, default=False): Preview mode - shows what would happen without subscribing
   177→
   178→    **Example**:
   179→    ```
   180→    # Ask your agent:
   181→    "Read my package.json and auto-subscribe to all dependencies"
   182→    "Index all the libraries from my requirements.txt"
   183→    "Use auto_subscribe_dependencies on my Cargo.toml"
   184→    ```
   185→
   186→    <Info>
   187→      This feature indexes 10-100 dependencies at a time. Best suited for **Pro & Startup plans** due to indexing volume.
   188→    </Info>
   189→  </Accordion>
   190→
   191→  <Accordion title="Universal Search">
   192→    ### `search`
   193→    Semantic search across indexed repositories, documentation, research papers, and local folders. Omit sources for universal hybrid search across all indexed content.
   194→
   195→    **Parameters**:
   196→    - `query` (str, required): Natural language search question
   197→    - `repositories` (List[str], optional): List of repositories to search (owner/repo format)
   198→    - `data_sources` (List[str], optional): Documentation/research paper sources (UUID, display_name, URL, or arXiv URL)
   199→    - `local_folders` (List[str], optional): Local folder IDs to search (private, searched separately)
   200→    - `category` (str, optional): Filter local folder results by classification category
   201→    - `search_mode` (str, default="unified"): Search scope - "unified", "repositories", or "sources"
   202→    - `include_sources` (bool, default=True): Include source code/document snippets
   203→
   204→    **Example**:
   205→    ```
   206→    # Universal search across everything
   207→    "Search for how authentication is implemented"
   208→
   209→    # Target specific sources
   210→    "Search the react repo for useEffect implementation"
   211→    ```
   212→
   213→    **Best for**: Searching across your indexed knowledge base with natural language.
   214→  </Accordion>
   215→
   216→  <Accordion title="Navigation & Reading Tools">
   217→    These tools work across all source types: repositories, documentation, packages, local folders, and HuggingFace datasets. The `source_type` parameter determines which source to target.
   218→
   219→    ### `nia_read`
   220→    Read content from any source type.
   221→
   222→    **Parameters**:
   223→    - `source_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "package", "local_folder", or "huggingface_dataset"
   224→    - For **repository**: `source_identifier` (owner/repo:path/to/file)
   225→    - For **documentation**: `doc_source_id` (UUID, display_name, or URL) + `path`
   226→    - For **package**: `registry`, `package_name`, `filename_sha256`, `start_line`, `end_line`
   227→    - For **local_folder**: `local_folder_id` + `path`
   228→    - `line_start` / `line_end` (int, optional): Line range
   229→    - `max_tokens` (int, optional): Maximum tokens to return
   230→
   231→    ### `nia_grep`
   232→    Regex search in any source type.
   233→
   234→    **Parameters**:
   235→    - `source_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "package", "local_folder", or "huggingface_dataset"
   236→    - `pattern` (str, required): Regex pattern to search for
   237→    - For **repository**: `repository` (owner/repo format)
   238→    - For **documentation**: `doc_source_id` (UUID, display_name, or URL)
   239→    - For **package**: `registry`, `package_name`
   240→    - For **local_folder**: `local_folder_id`
   241→    - `path` (str, optional): Path prefix to limit search scope
   242→    - `case_sensitive` (bool, default=False): Case-sensitive matching
   243→    - `context_lines` (int, optional): Lines of context around matches
   244→    - `output_mode` (str, default="content"): "content", "files_with_matches", or "count"
   245→
   246→    ### `nia_explore`
   247→    Browse file structure of any source type.
   248→
   249→    **Parameters**:
   250→    - `source_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "local_folder", or "huggingface_dataset"
   251→    - `action` (str, default="tree"): "tree" for full structure, "ls" for directory listing
   252→    - For **repository**: `repository` (owner/repo format)
   253→    - For **documentation**: `doc_source_id`
   254→    - For **local_folder**: `local_folder_id`
   255→    - `path` (str, default="/"): Path for ls action
   256→
   257→    **Examples**:
   258→    ```
   259→    # Read from a repository
   260→    nia_read(source_type="repository", source_identifier="vercel/ai-sdk:src/index.ts")
   261→
   262→    # Grep in documentation
   263→    nia_grep(source_type="documentation", doc_source_id="nextjs-docs", pattern="getServerSideProps")
   264→
   265→    # Explore local folder structure
   266→    nia_explore(source_type="local_folder", local_folder_id="my-notes", action="tree")
   267→    ```
   268→  </Accordion>
   269→
   270→  <Accordion title="Resource Management">
   271→    ### `manage_resource`
   272→    Unified resource management tool for repositories, documentation, research papers, HuggingFace datasets, and local folders.
   273→
   274→    **Actions**:
   275→
   276→    #### Action: "list"
   277→    List all indexed resources with their status.
   278→
   279→    **Parameters**:
   280→    - `action` (str, required): "list"
   281→    - `resource_type` (str, optional): Filter by "repository", "documentation", "research_paper", "huggingface_dataset", "local_folder", or None for all
   282→    - `query` (str, optional): Text filter (matches repo/display_name/url/title/id)
   283→    - `limit` (int, default=10): Maximum items to return per section (1-100)
   284→    - `offset` (int, default=0): Pagination offset
   285→    - `view` (str, default="auto"): Output detail level - "auto", "summary", "compact", "detailed"
   286→    - `show_all` (bool, default=False): Ignore limit/offset and show all matches
   287→
   288→    #### Action: "status"
   289→    Check indexing progress and status of any resource.
   290→
   291→    **Parameters**:
   292→    - `action` (str, required): "status"
   293→    - `resource_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "research_paper", "huggingface_dataset", or "local_folder"
   294→    - `identifier` (str, required): Repository name (owner/repo), documentation UUID/URL, paper identifier, or local folder ID
   295→
   296→    #### Action: "rename"
   297→    Rename a resource for better organization.
   298→
   299→    **Parameters**:
   300→    - `action` (str, required): "rename"
   301→    - `resource_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "research_paper", "huggingface_dataset", or "local_folder"
   302→    - `identifier` (str, required): Resource identifier (owner/repo, UUID/URL, or local folder ID)
   303→    - `new_name` (str, required): New display name (1-100 characters)
   304→
   305→    #### Action: "delete"
   306→    Remove an indexed resource.
   307→
   308→    **Parameters**:
   309→    - `action` (str, required): "delete"
   310→    - `resource_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "research_paper", "huggingface_dataset", or "local_folder"
   311→    - `identifier` (str, required): Resource identifier (owner/repo, UUID/URL, or local folder ID)
   312→
   313→    #### Action: "subscribe"
   314→    Subscribe to a globally indexed public source. If the source is already indexed by others, you get instant access without re-indexing.
   315→
   316→    **Parameters**:
   317→    - `action` (str, required): "subscribe"
   318→    - `resource_type` (str, required): "repository", "documentation", "research_paper", or "huggingface_dataset"
   319→    - `identifier` (str, required): URL of the source to subscribe to
   320→
   321→    **Example**:
   322→    ```
   323→    manage_resource(action='subscribe', identifier='https://github.com/vercel/ai-sdk', resource_type='repository')
   324→    ```
   325→
   326→    **Identifier Formats**:
   327→    - For repositories: owner/repo format (e.g., "facebook/react") or GitHub URL
   328→    - For documentation: UUID preferred, also supports display name or URL
   329→    - For research papers: UUID or arXiv abs/pdf URL
   330→    - For HuggingFace datasets: UUID or dataset_id (e.g., "openai/gsm8k", "squad")
   331→    - For local folders: UUID or display name
   332→  </Accordion>
   333→
   334→  <Accordion title="Research & Analysis Tools">
   335→    ### `nia_research`
   336→    AI-powered research with three modes for different use cases.
   337→
   338→    **Modes**:
   339→
   340→    | Mode | Description | Best For |
   341→    |------|-------------|----------|
   342→    | `quick` | Fast web search | Finding repos, docs, quick answers |
   343→    | `deep` | AI agent research | Comparisons, evaluations, analysis |
   344→    | `oracle` | Full autonomous research | Complex multi-step investigations |
   345→
   346→    **Parameters**:
   347→    - `query` (str, required): Research question
   348→    - `mode` (str, default="quick"): "quick", "deep", or "oracle"
   349→
   350→    **Examples**:
   351→    ```
   352→    # Quick web search
   353→    "Use nia_research quick to find the best GraphRAG frameworks"
   354→
   355→    # Deep analysis
   356→    "Use nia_research deep to compare Prisma vs Drizzle"
   357→
   358→    # Full autonomous research
   359→    "Use nia_research oracle to investigate authentication best practices for Next.js"
   360→    ```
   361→
   362→    ### `nia_advisor`
   363→    Analyze your code against documentation for tailored recommendations. Compares your implementation with best practices from indexed docs.
   364→
   365→    **Parameters**:
   366→    - `code` (str, required): Your code to analyze
   367→    - `doc_source_id` (str, required): Documentation source to compare against
   368→    - `question` (str, optional): Specific aspect to focus on
   369→
   370→    **Example**:
   371→    ```
   372→    # Ask your agent:
   373→    "Use nia_advisor to check my authentication code against the BetterAuth docs"
   374→    "Analyze my React component against the Next.js App Router documentation"
   375→    ```
   376→
   377→    **Best for**: Getting documentation-grounded recommendations for improving your code.
   378→  </Accordion>
   379→
   380→  <Accordion title="Context Sharing">
   381→    ### `context`
   382→    Cross-agent context sharing for saving, retrieving, and managing conversation contexts.
   383→
   384→    **Memory Types**:
   385→
   386→    | Type | Duration | Best For |
   387→    |------|----------|----------|
   388→    | `scratchpad` | 1 hour | Temporary working memory |
   389→    | `episodic` | 7 days | Session-based context |
   390→    | `fact` | Permanent | Learned facts about the codebase |
   391→    | `procedural` | Permanent | How-to knowledge and procedures |
   392→
   393→    **Actions**:
   394→
   395→    #### Action: "save"
   396→    Save conversation context.
   397→
   398→    **Parameters**:
   399→    - `action` (str, required): "save"
   400→    - `title` (str, required): Descriptive title
   401→    - `summary` (str, required): Brief summary
   402→    - `content` (str, required): Full conversation context
   403→    - `agent_source` (str, required): Which agent created this (e.g., "cursor", "claude-code")
   404→    - `memory_type` (str, optional): "scratchpad", "episodic", "fact", or "procedural"
   405→    - `tags` (List[str], optional): Searchable tags
   406→    - `edited_files` (List[dict], optional): Files modified during conversation
   407→
   408→    #### Action: "list"
   409→    List saved contexts with optional filtering.
   410→
   411→    #### Action: "retrieve"
   412→    Get full context by ID.
   413→
   414→    #### Action: "search" / "semantic-search" / "keyword-search"
   415→    Search contexts using different strategies.
   416→
   417→    #### Action: "update"
   418→    Update existing context.
   419→
   420→    #### Action: "delete"
   421→    Delete a context.
   422→
   423→    **Example**:
   424→    ```
   425→    # Save context for later
   426→    "Save this conversation about authentication setup"
   427→
   428→    # Retrieve relevant context
   429→    "Search my saved contexts for database migrations"
   430→    ```
   431→  </Accordion>
   432→</AccordionGroup>

1→---
     2→title: 'Tracer'
     3→description: 'Autonomous agent for searching GitHub repositories without indexing'
     4→---
     5→
     6→<Info>
     7→  **Pro Feature** - Tracer is available on Pro plans.
     8→</Info>
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→Tracer is an autonomous sub-agent that searches code on GitHub without requiring you to index repositories first. Powered by Claude Opus 4.6 with 1M context window support, it explores codebases, reads files, and synthesizes findings into comprehensive reports.
    13→
    14→Use Tracer when you need to quickly search unfamiliar code, find implementation examples, or understand how libraries work internally.
    15→
    16→<Frame>
    17→  <iframe
    18→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/FMYq_-alEMY"
    19→    title="Tracer Demo"
    20→    className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
    21→    allowFullScreen
    22→  />
    23→</Frame>
    24→
    25→## How It Works
    26→
    27→You provide a question and repositories to search. Tracer handles the rest:
    28→
    29→<Steps>
    30→  <Step title="Explore">
    31→    Browse repository structure to understand the layout
    32→  </Step>
    33→  <Step title="Search">
    34→    Find relevant code using GitHub's Code Search API
    35→  </Step>
    36→  <Step title="Read">
    37→    Analyze specific files and line ranges in detail
    38→  </Step>
    39→  <Step title="Iterate">
    40→    Follow code paths as search results reveal new areas to explore
    41→  </Step>
    42→  <Step title="Synthesize">
    43→    Generate a comprehensive report with file paths and line citations
    44→  </Step>
    45→</Steps>
    46→
    47→## Available Tools
    48→
    49→Tracer autonomously uses four GitHub tools:
    50→
    51→| Tool | Purpose |
    52→|------|---------|
    53→| `github_search` | Code search with qualifiers (`language:`, `path:`) |
    54→| `github_list` | Browse file tree structure |
    55→| `github_read` | Read file contents with optional line ranges |
    56→| `github_glob` | Find files matching glob patterns |
    57→
    58→## API Usage
    59→
    60→### Create a Tracer Job
    61→
    62→```bash
    63→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/github/tracer \
    64→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
    65→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    66→  -d '{
    67→    "query": "How does the streaming response work in generateText?",
    68→    "repositories": ["vercel/ai"],
    69→    "context": "Focus on the core streaming implementation, not the React hooks."
    70→  }'
    71→```
    72→
    73→**Parameters:**
    74→
    75→| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
    76→|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
    77→| `query` | string | Yes | Your research question |
    78→| `repositories` | string[] | No | Repositories to search (owner/repo format) |
    79→| `context` | string | No | Additional context to guide the agent's focus |
    80→
    81→**Response:**
    82→
    83→```json
    84→{
    85→  "job_id": "abc123",
    86→  "session_id": "def456",
    87→  "status": "queued"
    88→}
    89→```
    90→
    91→### Stream Results
    92→
    93→Subscribe to real-time updates as Tracer works:
    94→
    95→```bash
    96→curl -N https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/github/tracer/{job_id}/stream \
    97→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
    98→```
    99→
   100→**Event Types:**
   101→
   102→| Event | Description |
   103→|-------|-------------|
   104→| `started` | Job started with query and repositories |
   105→| `tool_start` | Tool execution beginning |
   106→| `tool_complete` | Tool finished with success/failure |
   107→| `complete` | Final report ready |
   108→| `error` | Error occurred |
   109→
   110→### Get Job Status
   111→
   112→```bash
   113→curl https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/github/tracer/{job_id} \
   114→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   115→```
   116→
   117→### List Jobs
   118→
   119→```bash
   120→curl https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/github/tracer \
   121→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   122→```
   123→
   124→## MCP Integration
   125→
   126→Tracer is available as an MCP tool. Once configured, use it in Claude Code, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible agent:
   127→
   128→```
   129→"Use Tracer to search the vercel/ai repository and explain how streaming works."
   130→```
   131→
   132→## Example Use Cases
   133→
   134→<Accordion title="Understanding Library Internals">
   135→  **Query:** "Explain why we're getting this error from Zod and show me the validation logic causing it."
   136→
   137→  Tracer searches the Zod repo for error messages, finds the validation functions, reads the implementation, and explains exactly what triggers the error.
   138→</Accordion>
   139→
   140→<Accordion title="Finding Implementation Examples">
   141→  **Query:** "Find examples of how to implement cursor-based pagination in GraphQL resolvers."
   142→
   143→  Tracer searches multiple repositories, finds pagination implementations, compares approaches, and synthesizes findings.
   144→</Accordion>
   145→
   146→<Accordion title="Learning Framework Patterns">
   147→  **Query:** "How does React's useEffect cleanup function work?"
   148→
   149→  Tracer navigates the React codebase, finds the hooks implementation, reads the relevant code, and explains the cleanup mechanism.
   150→</Accordion>
   151→
   152→## Tracer vs Index-Based Search
   153→
   154→| Aspect | Tracer | Nia Search |
   155→|--------|--------|------------|
   156→| **Speed** | Slower (live API calls) | Faster (pre-indexed) |
   157→| **Setup** | None | Requires indexing |
   158→| **Coverage** | All public GitHub | Only indexed sources |
   159→| **Best for** | Exploration, one-off queries | Repeated searches, production use |
   160→
   161→**Use Tracer when:**
   162→- Exploring unfamiliar code
   163→- Searching repos you haven't indexed
   164→- Researching across multiple repositories
   165→
   166→**Use Nia Search when:**
   167→- Working with familiar codebases
   168→- Need fast, repeated searches
   169→- Want precise control over search scope
   170→

1→---
     2→title: 'Oracle Research Agent'
     3→description: 'Autonomous research assistant for deep technical investigations'
     4→---
     5→
     6→<Info>
     7→  **Pro Feature** - Oracle Research Agent is available exclusively on Pro plans.
     8→</Info>
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→Oracle Research Agent is Nia's autonomous research assistant that conducts deep investigations across codebases, documentation, and the web. It serves as a specialized "second brain" for external knowledge needs and technical research.
    13→
    14→<Frame>
    15→  <iframe
    16→    width="100%"
    17→    height="400"
    18→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/DWBwUUhzhzE"
    19→    title="Oracle Research Agent Demo"
    20→    frameBorder="0"
    21→    allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture"
    22→    allowFullScreen
    23→  ></iframe>
    24→</Frame>
    25→
    26→## Key Capabilities
    27→
    28→Oracle is powered by the Nia API and includes:
    29→
    30→- **Web search** - Find information across the internet
    31→- **Code search** - Search through repository code
    32→- **Documentation search** - Query documentation sources
    33→- **Doc filesystem tools** - Navigate documentation with `doc_tree`, `doc_ls`, `doc_read`, `doc_grep`
    34→- **Package source code analysis** - Explore PyPI, NPM, Crates.io, Go modules
    35→- **Context preservation** - Organize findings for handoff to main agents
    36→
    37→## Research Strategy
    38→
    39→Oracle follows a progressive 3-step research pattern:
    40→
    41→<Steps>
    42→  <Step title="DISCOVER">
    43→    - Use `nia_web_search` for quick lookups and discovery
    44→    - Use `nia_deep_research_agent` for complex analysis, comparisons, and pros/cons evaluation
    45→  </Step>
    46→
    47→  <Step title="INDEX">
    48→    - Automatically index discovered resources
    49→    - Monitor status during indexing process
    50→  </Step>
    51→
    52→  <Step title="SEARCH">
    53→    - Use `search_codebase`, `search_documentation`, `regex_search`
    54→    - Use `read_source_content` for specific file analysis
    55→  </Step>
    56→</Steps>
    57→
    58→## Execution Methods
    59→
    60→### 1. Direct Streaming (Real-time)
    61→
    62→Use the `/v2/oracle` endpoint with SSE streaming for immediate results:
    63→
    64→```bash
    65→curl --request POST \
    66→  --url https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/oracle \
    67→  --header 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
    68→  --header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    69→  --data '{
    70→    "query": "How does authentication work in the FastAPI codebase?",
    71→    "repositories": ["fastapi/fastapi"],
    72→    "data_sources": ["FastAPI Documentation"],
    73→    "output_format": "markdown report with code examples",
    74→    "model": "claude-opus-4-5-20251101"
    75→  }'
    76→```
    77→
    78→### 2. Job-based Execution (Recommended)
    79→
    80→Create asynchronous jobs via Hatchet workflow for better reliability:
    81→
    82→**Benefits:**
    83→- Queues jobs for reliable execution
    84→- Supports concurrency limits per user
    85→- Enables reconnection to running jobs
    86→- Provides job status tracking and history
    87→
    88→**Workflow:**
    89→1. Create job: `POST /v2/oracle/jobs`
    90→2. Subscribe to updates: `GET /v2/oracle/jobs/{job_id}/stream` (SSE)
    91→3. Check status: `GET /v2/oracle/jobs/{job_id}`
    92→
    93→## Real-time Event Types
    94→
    95→Both execution methods provide real-time streaming events:
    96→
    97→| Event Type | Description |
    98→|------------|-------------|
    99→| `iteration_start` | New research iteration beginning |
   100→| `tool_start` | Tool execution starting (includes action, args, reason) |
   101→| `tool_complete` | Tool finished with success/failure status |
   102→| `generating_report` | Final report synthesis starting |
   103→| `complete` | Research finished with full result |
   104→| `heartbeat` | Connection keep-alive (every 0.5s during idle) |
   105→| `error` | Error occurred during research |
   106→| `workflow_event` | Hatchet workflow state changes (job-based only) |
   107→
   108→## Example Workflows
   109→
   110→<Accordion title="Quick Discovery Example">
   111→  **User Query:** "Find popular JWT libraries for Python"
   112→
   113→  **Oracle Workflow:**
   114→  1. `nia_web_search("popular JWT libraries Python")`
   115→  2. Review results for top candidates
   116→  3. Auto-index top 2-3 repositories
   117→  4. `search_codebase` for implementation patterns
   118→  5. Present findings with sources
   119→</Accordion>
   120→
   121→## Additional Features
   122→
   123→- **Chat with results:** Follow up on research with `/v2/oracle/sessions/{session_id}/chat`
   124→- **View history:** List past sessions with `/v2/oracle/history`
   125→- **Session details:** Get full session info with `/v2/oracle/sessions/{session_id}`
   126→- **Message history:** Retrieve chat messages with `/v2/oracle/sessions/{session_id}/messages`
   127→
   128→## Response Format
   129→
   130→Completed research includes:
   131→- Final report (markdown or specified format)
   132→- Citations with source IDs and summaries
   133→- Tool call history
   134→- Iteration count and duration
   135→- Session ID for follow-ups
   136→
   137→

1→---
     2→title: 'API Guide'
     3→description: 'Authentication, rate limits, best practices, and integration guides for the Nia API'
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Authentication
     7→
     8→All API requests require authentication using an API key. Include your API key in the `Authorization` header:
     9→
    10→```bash
    11→Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY
    12→```
    13→
    14→Get your API key at [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai). 
    15→
    16→For autonomous agents, use [Agent Onboarding](/agent-onboarding) to create accounts and keys via API (`/v2/auth/signup`, `/v2/auth/bootstrap-key`, `/v2/auth/login-key`) and then install the skill non-interactively.
    17→
    18→<Warning>
    19→  Store your API key in an environment variable or secret manager. Rotate it immediately if compromised.
    20→</Warning>
    21→
    22→## Base URL
    23→
    24→All API endpoints are available at:
    25→
    26→```
    27→https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2
    28→```
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Rate Limits
    33→
    34→View your current usage and limits at [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai). See the [Pricing page](/pricing) for plan details.
    35→
    36→When you exceed rate limits, the API returns a `429` status code:
    37→
    38→```json
    39→{
    40→  "error": "Rate limit exceeded",
    41→  "status": 429
    42→}
    43→```
    44→
    45→Rate limit headers are included in responses:
    46→
    47→| Header | Description |
    48→|--------|-------------|
    49→| `X-RateLimit-Limit` | Rate limit ceiling for the endpoint |
    50→| `X-RateLimit-Remaining` | Remaining requests in current window |
    51→| `X-RateLimit-Reset` | Time when the rate limit resets |
    52→| `X-Monthly-Limit` | Monthly request limit |
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## Quick Start Examples
    57→
    58→### Index a Repository
    59→
    60→```bash
    61→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/repositories \
    62→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
    63→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    64→  -d '{"repository": "vercel/ai"}'
    65→```
    66→
    67→### Index Documentation
    68→
    69→```bash
    70→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/data-sources \
    71→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
    72→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    73→  -d '{"url": "https://sdk.vercel.ai/docs"}'
    74→```
    75→
    76→### Search Across Sources
    77→
    78→```bash
    79→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/universal-search \
    80→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
    81→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    82→  -d '{
    83→    "query": "How do I stream responses?",
    84→    "repositories": ["vercel/ai"],
    85→    "data_sources": ["Vercel AI SDK"]
    86→  }'
    87→```
    88→
    89→### Package Search (No Indexing Required)
    90→
    91→```bash
    92→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/package-search/grep \
    93→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
    94→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    95→  -d '{
    96→    "registry": "npm",
    97→    "package_name": "ai",
    98→    "pattern": "streamText"
    99→  }'
   100→```
   101→
   102→---
   103→
   104→## Best Practices
   105→
   106→### Use Universal Search for Multi-Source Queries
   107→
   108→Instead of querying repositories and documentation separately, use the `/universal-search` endpoint to search across all your indexed sources at once:
   109→
   110→```bash
   111→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/universal-search \
   112→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
   113→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   114→  -d '{
   115→    "query": "authentication middleware implementation",
   116→    "search_mode": "unified"
   117→  }'
   118→```
   119→
   120→### Leverage Package Search for Dependencies
   121→
   122→Search through 3,000+ packages across PyPI, NPM, Crates.io, and Go modules without indexing:
   123→
   124→```python
   125→import requests
   126→
   127→headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {NIA_API_KEY}"}
   128→
   129→# Semantic search for understanding patterns
   130→response = requests.post(
   131→    "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/package-search/hybrid",
   132→    headers=headers,
   133→    json={
   134→        "registry": "py_pi",
   135→        "package_name": "fastapi",
   136→        "semantic_queries": ["How does dependency injection work?"]
   137→    }
   138→)
   139→```
   140→
   141→### Handle Rate Limits with Exponential Backoff
   142→
   143→```python
   144→import time
   145→import requests
   146→
   147→def fetch_with_retry(url, headers, json_data, max_retries=3):
   148→    for attempt in range(max_retries):
   149→        response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=json_data)
   150→        
   151→        if response.status_code == 429:
   152→            wait_time = 2 ** attempt
   153→            print(f"Rate limited. Waiting {wait_time}s...")
   154→            time.sleep(wait_time)
   155→            continue
   156→        
   157→        return response
   158→    
   159→    raise Exception("Max retries exceeded")
   160→```
   161→
   162→### Monitor Indexing Progress
   163→
   164→Large repositories take time to index. Poll the status endpoint:
   165→
   166→```bash
   167→# Check repository indexing status
   168→curl https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/repositories/vercel%2Fai \
   169→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   170→```
   171→
   172→Response includes progress:
   173→
   174→```json
   175→{
   176→  "repository": "vercel/ai",
   177→  "status": "indexing",
   178→  "progress": {
   179→    "percentage": 45,
   180→    "stage": "embedding",
   181→    "message": "Processing files..."
   182→  }
   183→}
   184→```
   185→
   186→---
   187→
   188→## Error Handling
   189→
   190→The Nia API uses standard HTTP status codes:
   191→
   192→| Code | Description | Action |
   193→|------|-------------|--------|
   194→| 200 | Success | Process the response normally |
   195→| 400 | Bad Request | Check request parameters |
   196→| 401 | Unauthorized | Verify your API key |
   197→| 404 | Not Found | Resource doesn't exist |
   198→| 429 | Rate Limited | Implement backoff and retry |
   199→| 500 | Server Error | Retry with backoff, contact support if persistent |
   200→
   201→### Error Response Format
   202→
   203→```json
   204→{
   205→  "error": "Error message describing what went wrong",
   206→  "status": 429
   207→}
   208→```
   209→
   210→---
   211→
   212→## SDK & Integration Options
   213→
   214→### MCP Server (Recommended for AI Agents)
   215→
   216→The easiest way to integrate Nia with AI coding agents:
   217→
   218→```bash
   219→curl -fsSL https://app.trynia.ai/cli | sh
   220→```
   221→
   222→See the [Installation guide](/integrations/installation/overview) for detailed setup.
   223→
   224→### Direct API Integration
   225→
   226→For custom applications, use the REST API directly:
   227→
   228→**JavaScript/TypeScript:**
   229→
   230→```javascript
   231→const response = await fetch("https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/universal-search", {
   232→  method: "POST",
   233→  headers: {
   234→    "Authorization": `Bearer ${process.env.NIA_API_KEY}`,
   235→    "Content-Type": "application/json"
   236→  },
   237→  body: JSON.stringify({
   238→    query: "How does error handling work?",
   239→    repositories: ["fastapi/fastapi"]
   240→  })
   241→});
   242→
   243→const results = await response.json();
   244→```
   245→
   246→**Python:**
   247→
   248→```python
   249→import requests
   250→
   251→headers = {
   252→    "Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['NIA_API_KEY']}",
   253→    "Content-Type": "application/json"
   254→}
   255→
   256→response = requests.post(
   257→    "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/universal-search",
   258→    headers=headers,
   259→    json={
   260→        "query": "How does error handling work?",
   261→        "repositories": ["fastapi/fastapi"]
   262→    }
   263→)
   264→
   265→results = response.json()
   266→```
   267→
   268→---
   269→
   270→## Key Endpoints
   271→
   272→| Endpoint | Description |
   273→|----------|-------------|
   274→| `POST /repositories` | Index a GitHub repository |
   275→| `POST /data-sources` | Index documentation/website |

   286→<Info>
   287→  Check out the [API Reference](/api-reference) for complete endpoint documentation with request/response schemas.

1→---
     2→title: "Agent Onboarding"
     3→description: "API-first account creation and non-interactive skill installation for autonomous agents"
     4→---
     5→
     6→Use this guide when an AI agent needs to onboard without browser prompts.
     7→
     8→## Prerequisites
     9→
    10→- Base URL: `https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2`
    11→- `nia-wizard` installed or runnable with `npx`.
    12→
    13→## Flow Overview
    14→
    15→1. Create account and bootstrap session token with `POST /v2/auth/signup`.
    16→2. Exchange bootstrap token for an API key with `POST /v2/auth/bootstrap-key`.
    17→3. Install Nia skill in non-interactive mode using `nia-wizard skill add`.
    18→
    19→For returning users, skip steps 1-2 and call `POST /v2/auth/login-key`.
    20→
    21→## Create Account (API-First)
    22→
    23→```bash
    24→curl -sS -X POST "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/auth/signup" \
    25→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    26→  -d '{
    27→    "email": "agent@example.com",
    28→    "password": "strong-password-123",
    29→    "organization_name": "Agent Org",
    30→    "first_name": "Agent",
    31→    "last_name": "Runner",
    32→    "idempotency_key": "signup-agent-001"
    33→  }'
    34→```
    35→
    36→Response includes:
    37→- `bootstrap_token`
    38→- `expires_at`
    39→- `user_id`
    40→- `organization_id`
    41→
    42→## Exchange Bootstrap Token For API Key
    43→
    44→```bash
    45→curl -sS -X POST "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/auth/bootstrap-key" \
    46→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    47→  -d '{
    48→    "bootstrap_token": "<bootstrap-token>"
    49→  }'
    50→```
    51→
    52→Response includes:
    53→- `api_key`
    54→- `api_key_id`
    55→- `user_id`
    56→- `organization_id`
    57→
    58→## Returning User Login (Email + Password)
    59→
    60→```bash
    61→curl -sS -X POST "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/auth/login-key" \
    62→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    63→  -d '{
    64→    "email": "agent@example.com",
    65→    "password": "strong-password-123",
    66→    "organization_id": "<optional-org-id>",
    67→    "idempotency_key": "login-agent-001"
    68→  }'
    69→```
    70→
    71→Response includes a fresh `api_key`.
    72→
    73→## Install Nia Skill Non-Interactively
    74→
    75→```bash
    76→npx nia-wizard skill add \
    77→  --api-key "<api-key-from-auth-endpoint>" \
    78→  --source nozomio-labs/nia-skill \
    79→  --non-interactive \
    80→  --ci
    81→```
    82→
    83→Optional target pinning:
    84→
    85→```bash
    86→npx nia-wizard skill add \
    87→  --api-key "<api-key-from-auth-endpoint>" \
    88→  --target codex \
    89→  --non-interactive \
    90→  --ci
    91→```
    92→
    93→## End-To-End Script (Headless)
    94→
    95→```bash
    96→#!/usr/bin/env bash
    97→set -euo pipefail
    98→
    99→BASE_URL="https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2"
   100→EMAIL="agent@example.com"
   101→PASSWORD="strong-password-123"
   102→ORG_NAME="Agent Org"
   103→
   104→SIGNUP_JSON=$(curl -sS -X POST "$BASE_URL/auth/signup" \
   105→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   106→  -d "{\"email\":\"$EMAIL\",\"password\":\"$PASSWORD\",\"organization_name\":\"$ORG_NAME\"}")
   107→
   108→BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN=$(echo "$SIGNUP_JSON" | jq -r '.bootstrap_token')
   109→
   110→KEY_JSON=$(curl -sS -X POST "$BASE_URL/auth/bootstrap-key" \
   111→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   112→  -d "{\"bootstrap_token\":\"$BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN\"}")
   113→
   114→API_KEY=$(echo "$KEY_JSON" | jq -r '.api_key')
   115→
   116→npx nia-wizard skill add --api-key "$API_KEY" --non-interactive --ci
   117→```
   118→
   119→<Warning>
   120→Handle credentials and generated API keys as secrets. Do not print them to shared logs.
   121→</Warning>
   122→
   123→## Reference
   124→
   125→See API Reference for these endpoints:
   126→- `POST /auth/signup`
   127→- `POST /auth/bootstrap-key`
   128→- `POST /auth/login-key`
   129→

1→---
     2→title: 'Local Sync'
     3→description: 'Continuously sync local folders, databases, and chat history to Nia.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→Nia Sync is a standalone CLI daemon that continuously synchronizes your local data sources with Nia, enabling AI agents to search your personal knowledge base.
     7→
     8→<Frame>
     9→  <iframe
    10→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/PKBNNAJXwxs"
    11→    title="Local Sync Demo"
    12→    className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
    13→    allowFullScreen
    14→  />
    15→</Frame>
    16→
    17→<Info>
    18→  **Privacy First**: Local folders are private to your account. They're searched separately from public repositories and documentation.
    19→</Info>
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→## Quick Install
    24→
    25→```bash
    26→curl -fsSL https://app.trynia.ai/install-sync | bash
    27→```
    28→
    29→Or install via pip:
    30→
    31→```bash
    32→pip install nia-sync
    33→```
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→## Getting Started
    38→
    39→<Steps>
    40→  <Step title="Login">
    41→    Authenticate with your Nia account:
    42→    ```bash
    43→    nia login
    44→    ```
    45→    This opens your browser for secure OAuth authentication.
    46→  </Step>
    47→
    48→  <Step title="Add a Source">
    49→    Add a local folder or database to sync:
    50→    ```bash
    51→    nia add ~/Documents/notes
    52→    ```
    53→    The CLI auto-detects known database paths (iMessage, browser history, etc.).
    54→  </Step>
    55→
    56→  <Step title="Start Syncing">
    57→    Start the sync daemon:
    58→    ```bash
    59→    nia
    60→    ```
    61→    The daemon watches for file changes and syncs incrementally in real-time.
    62→  </Step>
    63→
    64→  <Step title="Check Status">
    65→    View all synced sources:
    66→    ```bash
    67→    nia status
    68→    ```
    69→  </Step>
    70→</Steps>
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→## CLI Commands
    75→
    76→### Core Commands
    77→
    78→| Command | Description |
    79→|---------|-------------|
    80→| `nia` | Start sync daemon (watch mode by default) |
    81→| `nia login` | Browser-based OAuth authentication |
    82→| `nia logout` | Clear stored credentials |
    83→| `nia status` | Show all configured sources with sync status |
    84→| `nia status --json` | Output status as JSON for scripting |
    85→| `nia once` | Run one-time sync then exit |
    86→| `nia add <path>` | Add new source for sync |
    87→| `nia link <ID> <path>` | Link cloud source to local path |
    88→| `nia remove <ID>` | Remove source from sync |
    89→| `nia upgrade` | Check and install updates |
    90→
    91→### Search & Query
    92→
    93→Search your indexed sources directly from the terminal:
    94→
    95→```bash
    96→# Basic search with streaming AI response
    97→nia search "meeting notes from last week"
    98→
    99→# Search specific folders
   100→nia search "project updates" --local-folder my-notes --local-folder work-docs
   101→
   102→# Show source snippets alongside the answer
   103→nia search "authentication code" --sources
   104→
   105→# Raw text output (no markdown rendering)
   106→nia search "api endpoints" --no-markdown
   107→
   108→# JSON output for scripting
   109→nia search "config values" --json --no-stream
   110→```
   111→
   112→| Flag | Description |
   113→|------|-------------|
   114→| `-l, --local-folder` | Limit search to specific folder(s). Accepts ID prefix or display name |
   115→| `--sources` | Show source snippets used to generate the answer |
   116→| `--markdown/--no-markdown` | Toggle rich Markdown rendering (default: on) |
   117→| `--stream/--no-stream` | Toggle streaming response (default: on) |
   118→| `-j, --json` | Output raw JSON for programmatic use |
   119→| `-n, --limit` | Max source snippets to display |
   120→
   121→### Monitoring & Debugging
   122→
   123→| Command | Description |
   124→|---------|-------------|
   125→| `nia info <ID>` | Show detailed info for a source (chunk count, last sync, errors) |
   126→| `nia logs` | Show sync logs for all sources |
   127→| `nia logs <ID>` | Show sync logs for a specific source |
   128→| `nia logs --tail` | Continuously tail new logs |
   129→| `nia logs --errors` | Only show error logs |
   130→| `nia diff` | Dry-run: show what would sync without uploading |
   131→| `nia diff <ID>` | Dry-run for a specific source |
   132→| `nia doctor` | Run diagnostics for common issues (auth, API, disk access) |
   133→| `nia whoami` | Show current authenticated user |
   134→| `nia version` | Show CLI version |
   135→| `nia version --check` | Check for available updates |
   136→
   137→### Sync Control
   138→
   139→| Command | Description |
   140→|---------|-------------|
   141→| `nia pause <ID>` | Pause continuous sync for a source |
   142→| `nia resume <ID>` | Resume continuous sync for a source |
   143→| `nia resync <ID>` | Force full resync by resetting cursor |
   144→| `nia resync --all` | Force full resync for all sources |
   145→
   146→### Web Integration
   147→
   148→Open Nia web app pages directly from the CLI:
   149→
   150→```bash
   151→nia open dashboard      # Open main dashboard
   152→nia open activity       # Open activity feed
   153→nia open local-sync     # Open local sync settings
   154→nia open api-keys       # Open API keys page
   155→nia open billing        # Open billing page
   156→nia open docs           # Open documentation
   157→nia open <source-id>    # Open specific source detail page
   158→```
   159→
   160→---
   161→
   162→## Configuration Management
   163→
   164→### Config Commands
   165→
   166→Manage CLI settings directly from the terminal:
   167→
   168→```bash
   169→# View all config
   170→nia config list
   171→
   172→# Get a specific value
   173→nia config get api_url
   174→
   175→# Set a value
   176→nia config set api_url "https://api.trynia.ai"
   177→```
   178→
   179→### Ignore Patterns
   180→
   181→Customize which files are excluded from sync:
   182→
   183→```bash
   184→# List current ignore patterns
   185→nia ignore list
   186→
   187→# Ignore a directory name
   188→nia ignore add --dir node_modules
   189→
   190→# Ignore a file name
   191→nia ignore add --file .DS_Store
   192→
   193→# Ignore a file extension
   194→nia ignore add --ext .log
   195→
   196→# Ignore a path substring
   197→nia ignore add --path /build/
   198→
   199→# Remove an ignore pattern
   200→nia ignore remove --ext .log
   201→```
   202→
   203→### Watch Directories
   204→
   205→Configure which directories are scanned for auto-linking sources:
   206→
   207→```bash
   208→# List watched directories
   209→nia watch list
   210→
   211→# Add a directory to watch
   212→nia watch add ~/Projects
   213→
   214→# Remove a watch directory
   215→nia watch remove ~/Projects
   216→```
   217→
   218→<Tip>
   219→When you create a folder in a watched directory that matches an unlinked source's name, Nia automatically discovers and links it.
   220→</Tip>
   221→
   222→---
   223→
   224→## Source ID Shortcuts
   225→
   226→You don't need to type full UUIDs. Nia accepts:
   227→
   228→- **ID prefixes**: `nia info a3f2` matches `a3f2b1c8-...`
   229→- **Display names**: `nia pause "My Notes"` matches by folder name
   230→
   231→If a prefix is ambiguous (matches multiple sources), you'll be prompted with the matches.
   232→
   233→---
   234→
   235→## Daemon Mode
   236→
   237→Running `nia` without arguments starts the sync daemon with real-time file watching:
   238→
   239→```
   240→NIA Nia Sync Engine
   241→
   242→● real-time file watching
   243→● 3 source(s) active
   244→● Auto-refresh every 30s
   245→
   246→Ctrl+C to stop
   247→```
   248→
   249→The daemon:
   250→- Watches for file changes using native filesystem events
   251→- Syncs changes within seconds of detection
   252→- Periodically refreshes the source list from the web UI
   253→- Auto-discovers new folders matching unlinked sources
   254→- Sends heartbeats to mark daemon as online
   255→
   256→### Daemon Flags
   257→
   258→| Flag | Default | Description |
   259→|------|---------|-------------|
   260→| `--watch/--poll` | `--watch` | Real-time watching vs interval polling |
   261→| `-f, --fallback` | 600 | Fallback sync interval in seconds (catches missed events) |
   262→| `-r, --refresh` | 30 | How often to check for new sources from web UI |
   263→
   264→---
   265→
   266→## Supported Data Sources
   267→
   268→Nia Sync automatically extracts and indexes content from various data sources:
   269→
   270→<CardGroup cols={2}>
   271→  <Card title="Chat & Messages" icon="message">
   272→    - **iMessage** (`~/Library/Messages/chat.db`)
   273→    - **Telegram** (JSON exports, ZIP files)
   274→  </Card>
   275→
   276→  <Card title="Browser History" icon="globe">
   277→    - **Safari** (`~/Library/Safari/History.db`)
   278→    - **Chrome** / **Brave** / **Edge**
   279→    - **Firefox**
   280→  </Card>
   281→
   282→  <Card title="Databases" icon="database">
   283→    - Generic **SQLite** databases
   284→    - Auto-extracts tables into searchable text
   285→  </Card>
   286→
   287→  <Card title="Folders" icon="folder">
   288→    - Regular folders with text files
   289→    - Code, notes, documentation
   290→  </Card>
   291→</CardGroup>
   292→
   293→### Virtual File Format
   294→
   295→Database content is extracted into virtual text files for semantic search:
   296→
   297→| Source | Virtual Path Format |
   298→|--------|---------------------|
   299→| iMessage | `messages/{contact}/{date}_{row_id}_{direction}.txt` |
   300→| Safari/Chrome/Firefox | `history/{domain}/{date}_{id}.txt` |
   301→| Telegram | `telegram/{chat_name}/{date}_{msg_id}.txt` |
   302→
   303→---
   304→
   305→## Web UI
   306→
   307→You can also manage synced sources from the Nia web app:
   308→
   309→1. Go to [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai)
   310→2. Navigate to **Settings → Local Sync**
   311→3. View sync status, add/remove sources, and configure sync intervals
   312→
   313→### Sync Intervals
   314→
   315→Configure how often sources are synced:
   316→
   317→| Interval | Description |
   318→|----------|-------------|
   319→| `5m` | Every 5 minutes |
   320→| `hourly` | Every hour |
   321→| `6h` | Every 6 hours |
   322→| `daily` | Once per day |
   323→
   324→---
   325→
   326→## Searching Local Folders
   327→
   328→Once synced, your local folders are searchable via MCP tools:
   329→
   330→```python
   331→# Search across local folders
   332→search(query="meeting notes from last week", local_folders=["folder-id-here"])
   333→
   334→# Read files from a local folder
   335→nia_read(source_type="local_folder", local_folder_id="folder-id-here", path="/notes/meeting.txt")
   336→
   337→# Grep in local folders
   338→nia_grep(source_type="local_folder", local_folder_id="folder-id-here", pattern="TODO")
   339→
   340→# Explore folder structure
   341→nia_explore(source_type="local_folder", local_folder_id="folder-id-here", action="tree")
   342→```
   343→
   344→<Note>
   345→  Local folders are private and searched separately from public repositories. Use `local_folders` parameter in `search()` to include them.
   346→</Note>
   347→
   348→---
   349→
   350→## Configuration
   351→
   352→### Environment Variables
   353→
   354→| Variable | Default | Description |
   355→|----------|---------|-------------|
   356→| `NIA_API_URL` | `https://api.trynia.ai` | Override API base URL |
   357→
   358→### Config File
   359→
   360→Credentials are stored in `~/.nia-sync/config.json` with secure permissions (mode 0600).
   361→
   362→---
   363→
   364→## Limits
   365→
   366→| Limit | Value |
   367→|-------|-------|
   368→| Max files per folder | 5,000 |
   369→| Max total upload size | 100 MB per folder |
   370→| Max individual file size | 5 MB |
   371→| Max database size | 1 GB |
   372→| Max rows per table | 100,000 |
   373→
   374→---
   375→
   376→## Security
   377→
   378→<Info>
   379→  **350+ exclusion patterns** automatically protect sensitive files from being synced.
   380→</Info>
   381→
   382→The following are **never synced**:
   383→
   384→- **Credentials**: `.env`, `.pem`, `.key`, SSH keys, `*credentials*`, `*secrets*`, `*token*`
   385→- **Version control**: `.git`, `.svn`
   386→- **Dependencies**: `node_modules`, `venv`, `__pycache__`
   387→- **Build outputs**: `dist/`, `build/`, `.next/`
   388→
   389→API keys are stored locally with user-only read/write permissions and are never logged or transmitted in plaintext.
   390→
   391→---
   392→
   393→<Warning>
   394→**Need Help?** Join our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/BBSwUMrrfn) or reach out through [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai/) for support.
   395→</Warning>
   396→

1→---
     2→title: 'PDF Indexing'
     3→description: 'Index and chat with any complex PDF using tree-guided hybrid search'
     4→---
     5→
     6→Index any PDF — research papers, SEC filings, legal documents, technical manuals — and have intelligent conversations with accurate, section-aware retrieval.
     7→
     8→<Frame>
     9→  <iframe
    10→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/uHl_gho2o6g"
    11→    title="PDF Indexing Demo"
    12→    className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
    13→    allowFullScreen
    14→  />
    15→</Frame>
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Tree-Guided Hybrid Search
    20→
    21→Traditional RAG systems split documents into flat chunks, losing structure and context. Nia invented a different approach:
    22→
    23→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    24→  <Card title="Document Trees" icon="sitemap">
    25→    PDFs are parsed into hierarchical structures — sections, subsections, figures, tables — preserving the document's logical organization.
    26→  </Card>
    27→
    28→  <Card title="Section-Level Indexing" icon="layer-group">
    29→    Each section is indexed with its position in the hierarchy, enabling precise retrieval that knows *where* information lives.
    30→  </Card>
    31→
    32→  <Card title="Hybrid Signals" icon="code-merge">
    33→    Combines vector embeddings with non-vector signals (headers, page numbers, cross-references) for high recall with minimal context bloat.
    34→  </Card>
    35→
    36→  <Card title="Hierarchical Traversal" icon="arrows-split-up-and-left">
    37→    Agents traverse documents as trees instead of scanning flat chunks, finding relevant sections faster and more accurately.
    38→  </Card>
    39→</CardGroup>
    40→
    41→<Info>
    42→  **Why it matters:** High recall across large PDF corpora without stuffing context windows with irrelevant chunks. [Read the technical deep-dive →](https://www.trynia.ai/blog/tree-reasoning-pdf-search)
    43→</Info>
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## Interactive Papers Playground
    48→
    49→Experience PDFs like never before at [app.trynia.ai/playground/papers](https://app.trynia.ai/playground/papers):
    50→
    51→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    52→  <Card title="PDF Viewer" icon="file-pdf">
    53→    View documents directly in the browser with a full-featured PDF viewer. Resizable panes let you read and chat side-by-side.
    54→  </Card>
    55→
    56→  <Card title="Document Tree" icon="folder-tree">
    57→    Navigate using an intelligent document tree. Jump to specific sections, figures, or equations instantly.
    58→  </Card>
    59→
    60→  <Card title="Session History" icon="clock-rotate-left">
    61→    Your conversations are saved. Return anytime to continue where you left off, or review past discussions.
    62→  </Card>
    63→
    64→  <Card title="LaTeX Rendering" icon="square-root-variable">
    65→    Full LaTeX support for mathematical expressions. Complex equations render beautifully in chat responses.
    66→  </Card>
    67→</CardGroup>
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## How It Works
    72→
    73→<Steps>
    74→  <Step title="Index a PDF">
    75→    Provide an arXiv URL, paper ID, or upload directly:
    76→    - *"Index https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345"*
    77→    - *"Index this SEC filing"*
    78→  </Step>
    79→  
    80→  <Step title="Tree Extraction">
    81→    Nia parses the PDF structure — sections, headers, figures, tables — building a navigable document tree.
    82→  </Step>
    83→  
    84→  <Step title="Chat with Context">
    85→    Ask questions and get answers grounded in specific sections, with citations pointing to exact locations.
    86→  </Step>
    87→</Steps>
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Use Cases
    92→
    93→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    94→  <Card title="Research Papers" icon="flask">
    95→    Index arXiv papers for literature reviews. Search across dozens of papers at once, compare methodologies, understand dense sections.
    96→  </Card>
    97→
    98→  <Card title="SEC Filings" icon="building-columns">
    99→    Navigate 10-Ks, 10-Qs, and proxy statements. Find specific disclosures, compare across years, extract financial data.
   100→  </Card>
   101→
   102→  <Card title="Legal Documents" icon="scale-balanced">
   103→    Index contracts, briefs, and regulations. Search for specific clauses, understand obligations, compare versions.
   104→  </Card>
   105→
   106→  <Card title="Technical Manuals" icon="book">
   107→    Index product documentation, API specs, or internal wikis. Get precise answers with section references.
   108→  </Card>
   109→</CardGroup>
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→## Quick Start
   114→
   115→```bash
   116→# In your AI coding agent, just say:
   117→"Index https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345"
   118→
   119→# Then ask questions:
   120→"What problem does this paper solve?"
   121→"Summarize section 3.2"
   122→"What are the limitations mentioned by the authors?"
   123→```
   124→
   125→Or use the [Papers Playground](https://app.trynia.ai/playground/papers) for a visual experience.
   126→
   127→---
   128→
   129→## Supported Sources
   130→
   131→| Source | Format | Example |
   132→|--------|--------|---------|
   133→| arXiv | URL or ID | `https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345` or `2401.12345` |
   134→| arXiv PDF | Direct PDF link | `https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.12345` |
   135→
   136→<Info>
   137→  Support for direct PDF uploads and more sources coming soon.
   138→</Info>

Browse thousands of pre-indexed sources and add them to your workspace instantly. 7→ 8→ 9→ 10→ 11→ 12→## How It Works 13→ 14→Instead of indexing heavy frameworks yourself and waiting for the job to complete, you can subscribe to sources that others have already indexed: 15→ 16→1. **Browse the library** at [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai) → Global Sources 17→2. **Search** for the repository, documentation, or paper you need 18→3. **Click to subscribe** — instant access, no indexing required 19→ 20→--- 21→ 22→## Popular Pre-indexed Sources 23→ 24→Some of the most subscribed sources include: 25→ 26→- **Chromium** — Full browser codebase indexed by folder 27→- **React / Next.js** — Framework source + documentation 28→- **FastAPI** — Python web framework with docs 29→- **Vercel AI SDK** — AI tooling source and guides 30→- **LangChain** — LLM orchestration framework 31→ 32→ 33→ Can't find what you need? Index it yourself and it becomes available for others to subscribe to. 34→ 35→ 36→--- 37→ 38→## Benefits 39→ 40→| Your Own Indexing | Pre-indexed Sources | 41→|-------------------|---------------------| 42→| Wait for job to complete | Instant access | 43→| Uses your indexing quota | Doesn't count against quota | 44→| You maintain updates | Community keeps it fresh | 45→ 46→--- 47→ 48→## Getting Started 49→ 50→ 51→ 52→ Go to [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai) and navigate to the Global Sources section 53→ 54→ 55→ 56→ Use the search bar to find specific projects, or browse by type (repos, docs, papers) 57→ 58→ 59→ 60→ Click on any source to add it to your workspace. It's immediately available for search. 61→ 62→ 63→ 64→ Use Nia's search tools to query across your subscribed sources 65→ 66→ 67→ 68→ 69→ Pre-indexed sources are public repositories and documentation. For private codebases, you'll need to index them yourself. 70→ 71→ 72→
