    17→## Getting Started
    18→
    19→1. Go to [app.trynia.ai/explore](https://app.trynia.ai/explore)
    20→2. Start asking questions about any technology, framework, or paper
    21→3. Nia searches across all pre-indexed sources and responds with accurate, cited answers
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## Features
    26→
    27→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    28→  <Card title="Universal Knowledge" icon="globe">
    29→    Search across all pre-indexed sources at once. Ask about React, FastAPI, LangChain, or any indexed framework without subscribing first.
    30→  </Card>
    31→
    32→  <Card title="Session History" icon="clock-rotate-left">
    33→    Your conversations are automatically saved. View, load, and delete previous sessions anytime.
    34→  </Card>
    35→
    36→  <Card title="Cited Responses" icon="quote-left">
    37→    Every answer includes source citations. Click through to see the exact code or documentation referenced.
    38→  </Card>
    39→
    40→  <Card title="Streaming Responses" icon="bolt">
    41→    Answers stream in real-time. No waiting for the full response to generate.
    42→  </Card>
    43→</CardGroup>
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## Use Cases
    48→
    49→### Quick Learning
    50→
    51→Ask questions about any technology without setting up anything:
    52→
    53→```
    54→"How does Next.js App Router handle data fetching?"
    55→"What's the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?"
    56→"How do I implement authentication with BetterAuth?"
    57→```
    58→
    59→### Cross-Framework Comparisons
    60→
    61→Compare approaches across different frameworks:
    62→
    63→```
    64→"How does error handling differ between FastAPI and Express?"
    65→"Compare state management in React vs Vue"
    66→"What are the pros and cons of Prisma vs Drizzle?"
    67→```
    68→
    69→### Research Exploration
    70→
    71→Query indexed research papers:
    72→
    73→```
    74→"What papers discuss attention mechanisms in transformers?"
    75→"Explain the key findings from recent RAG research"
    76→```
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Session Management
    81→
    82→Your explore conversations are organized into sessions:
    83→
    84→| Action | How |
    85→|--------|-----|
    86→| **View sessions** | Click the history icon in the explore interface |
    87→| **Load a session** | Click on any previous conversation to continue it |
    88→| **Delete a session** | Remove conversations you no longer need |
    89→| **Start fresh** | Begin a new session with a clean slate |
    90→
    91→<Info>
    92→  Sessions are tied to your account. Switch between devices and your history follows you.
    93→</Info>
    94→
    95→---
    96→
    97→## Explore vs. MCP Search
    98→
    99→| Explore Chat | MCP `search` Tool |
   100→|--------------|-------------------|
   101→| Web interface at app.trynia.ai | Works in your IDE/agent |
   102→| Searches all pre-indexed sources | Can target specific subscribed sources |
   103→| Visual session management | Programmatic access |
   104→| Great for learning & exploration | Great for coding workflows |
   105→
   106→Both use the same powerful search backend — choose based on your workflow.
   107→
   108→---
   109→
   110→## Tips
   111→
   112→<Tip>
   113→  **Be specific** — Instead of "how does React work?", try "how does React's reconciliation algorithm handle list updates?"
   114→</Tip>
   115→
   116→<Tip>
   117→  **Reference context** — Follow-up questions remember the conversation. Ask "can you show me an example?" after an explanation.
   118→</Tip>
   119→
   120→<Tip>
   121→  **Mix sources** — Ask questions that span multiple frameworks. Nia will pull from all relevant indexed sources.
   122→</Tip>
   123→

1→---
     2→title: 'Scoped MCP Servers'
     3→description: 'Spin up specialized MCP servers for specific knowledge sources'
     4→---
     5→
     6→Don't want a general-purpose MCP with dozens of tools? Create a scoped MCP that focuses on just the knowledge you need.
     7→
     8→<Frame>
     9→  <iframe
    10→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/zGmomjk5GDI"
    11→    title="Scoped MCP Demo"
    12→    className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
    13→    allowFullScreen
    14→  />
    15→</Frame>
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## What are Scoped MCPs?
    20→
    21→Instead of using Nia's full MCP server with all tools, you can generate a specialized MCP server that:
    22→
    23→- **Focuses on a single source** — One framework, one documentation site, one research paper
    24→- **Has fewer tools** — Only the tools relevant to that source
    25→- **Reduces context noise** — Your agent sees only what's relevant
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## How It Works
    30→
    31→<Steps>
    32→  <Step title="Pick a Pre-indexed Source">
    33→    Go to [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai) and browse the Global Sources. Find the documentation, repository, or paper you want.
    34→  </Step>
    35→
    36→  <Step title="Generate Scoped MCP">
    37→    Click on the source and select "Create Scoped MCP". Nia generates a dedicated MCP configuration for that source.
    38→  </Step>
    39→
    40→  <Step title="Add to Your IDE">
    41→    Copy the generated configuration into your IDE's MCP settings. The scoped MCP connects only to that specific knowledge source.
    42→  </Step>
    43→</Steps>
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## Example: Vercel AI SDK Scoped MCP
    48→
    49→Instead of querying all of Nia's knowledge, create an MCP that only knows about the Vercel AI SDK:
    50→
    51→```json
    52→{
    53→  "mcpServers": {
    54→    "nia-sdk-vercel-ai": {
    55→      "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp?source=https%3A%2F%2Fsdk.vercel.ai%2Fdocs",
    56→      "headers": {
    57→        "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
    58→      }
    59→    }
    60→  }
    61→}
    62→```
    63→
    64→The `source` parameter is URL-encoded. For `https://sdk.vercel.ai/docs`, it becomes `https%3A%2F%2Fsdk.vercel.ai%2Fdocs`.
    65→
    66→Now your agent has access to Vercel AI SDK documentation without being distracted by other sources.
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## Popular Scoped MCPs
    71→
    72→Some commonly requested scoped servers:
    73→
    74→| Source | Use Case |
    75→|--------|----------|
    76→| **Next.js** | React framework with App Router, Server Components |
    77→| **BetterAuth** | Authentication library for TypeScript |
    78→| **FastAPI** | Python web framework |
    79→| **Tailwind CSS** | Utility-first CSS framework |
    80→| **Prisma** | TypeScript ORM |
    81→| **LangChain** | LLM orchestration framework |
    82→
    83→<Info>
    84→  Any pre-indexed source can become a scoped MCP. If it's in Global Sources, you can create a focused server for it.
    85→</Info>
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## When to Use Scoped MCPs
    90→
    91→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    92→  <Card title="Use Scoped MCP When" icon="bullseye">
    93→    - Working on a single-framework project
    94→    - Want minimal tool clutter
    95→    - Need faster, more focused responses
    96→    - Prefer explicit control over context
    97→  </Card>
    98→
    99→  <Card title="Use Full Nia MCP When" icon="layer-group">
   100→    - Working across multiple technologies
   101→    - Need indexing and research tools
   102→    - Want to search your own repos/docs
   103→    - Building with diverse dependencies
   104→  </Card>
   105→</CardGroup>
   106→
   107→---
   108→
   109→## Multiple Scoped MCPs
   110→
   111→You can run multiple scoped MCPs simultaneously:
   112→
   113→```json
   114→{
   115→  "mcpServers": {
   116→    "nia-nextjs": {
   117→      "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp?source=https%3A%2F%2Fnextjs.org%2Fdocs",
   118→      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" }
   119→    },
   120→    "nia-prisma": {
   121→      "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp?source=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.prisma.io%2Fdocs",
   122→      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" }
   123→    },
   124→    "nia-tailwind": {
   125→      "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp?source=https%3A%2F%2Ftailwindcss.com%2Fdocs",
   126→      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" }
   127→    }
   128→  }
   129→}
   130→```
   131→
   132→Each MCP operates independently, giving you precise control over what knowledge your agent can access.
   133→
   134→<Tip>
   135→  To get the URL-encoded source, take the documentation URL and encode special characters: `/` becomes `%2F`, `:` becomes `%3A`.
   136→</Tip>
   137→
   138→---
   139→
   140→## Scoped vs. Subscribed Sources
   141→
   142→| Scoped MCP | Subscribed Source (Full MCP) |
   143→|------------|------------------------------|
   144→| Dedicated MCP server per source | Single MCP, multiple sources |
   145→| Separate tool namespace | Shared tools query all subscribed |
   146→| Best for focused workflows | Best for cross-source queries |
   147→| Multiple MCP configs needed | One config, manage sources in UI |
   148→
   149→<Tip>
   150→  **Start with the full Nia MCP** if you're exploring. Switch to scoped MCPs once you know exactly which sources you need for a project.
   151→</Tip>
   152→

1→---
     2→title: 'Agent Context Sharing'
     3→description: 'Finish planning in one agent, pick up execution in another, without losing the thread.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→Save entire conversation histories - code snippets, plans, decisions, referenced sources, and edited files. Re-open them in another chat or coding agent.
     7→
     8→Nia preserves what you searched and every query you made.
     9→
    10→<Frame>
    11→  <iframe
    12→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/vEv_BnZF0EQ?si=egQ37i8rQoj05Buy"
    13→    title="Context Sharing Demo"
    14→    className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
    15→    allowFullScreen
    16→  />
    17→</Frame>
    18→
    19→This makes it easy to move between different agents. Plan with Cursor and continue execution in Claude Code. All your context comes with you.
    20→
    21→### Unified Context Tool
    22→
    23→The `context` tool provides all context management operations through different actions:
    24→
    25→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    26→  <Card title="Action: save" icon="floppy-disk">
    27→    Save conversation with title, summary, content, tags, and edited files
    28→  </Card>
    29→
    30→  <Card title="Action: list" icon="list">
    31→    List saved contexts with filtering (workspace, directory, file overlap)
    32→  </Card>
    33→
    34→  <Card title="Action: retrieve" icon="arrow-down">
    35→    Pull full context by ID
    36→  </Card>
    37→
    38→  <Card title="Action: search" icon="magnifying-glass">
    39→    Search by keyword across title, summary, content, and tags
    40→  </Card>
    41→
    42→  <Card title="Action: update" icon="pencil">
    43→    Update title, summary, content, tags, or metadata
    44→  </Card>
    45→
    46→  <Card title="Action: delete" icon="trash">
    47→    Remove contexts when done
    48→  </Card>
    49→</CardGroup>
    50→
    51→All operations use the same `context` tool with different `action` parameters.
    52→
    53→### How It Works
    54→
    55→When you save a context, Nia captures:
    56→- Your entire conversation history
    57→- Code snippets and files you edited
    58→- Plans and decisions you made
    59→- All the sources you referenced
    60→- Everything Nia searched for you
    61→- Every query you made
    62→
    63→When you open it in another agent, everything is there. No context lost.
    64→
    65→---
    66→
    67→Visit [trynia.ai](https://trynia.ai) to get started.

1→---
     2→title: 'HuggingFace Datasets'
     3→description: 'Index and search HuggingFace datasets for semantic retrieval in your AI workflows.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Overview
     7→
     8→Nia supports indexing [HuggingFace datasets](https://huggingface.co/datasets) for semantic and agentic search. This enables your AI agents to query dataset contents, understand schema structures, and retrieve relevant rows using natural language.
     9→
    10→Try it now in the [Interactive Playground →](https://app.trynia.ai/playground/datasets)
    11→
    12→<Frame>
    13→  <iframe
    14→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Du7iXmk_Ar0"
    15→    title="HuggingFace Datasets Demo"
    16→    className="w-full aspect-video rounded-xl"
    17→    allowFullScreen
    18→  ></iframe>
    19→</Frame>
    20→
    21→## Quick Start
    22→
    23→<Steps>
    24→  <Step title="Index a Dataset">
    25→    Ask your coding agent to index a HuggingFace dataset:
    26→    ```
    27→    "Index https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k"
    28→    "Index the squad dataset from HuggingFace"
    29→    ```
    30→  </Step>
    31→
    32→  <Step title="Search the Dataset">
    33→    Once indexed, search with natural language:
    34→    ```
    35→    "Search the gsm8k dataset for math problems about fractions"
    36→    "Find examples in squad about historical events"
    37→    ```
    38→  </Step>
    39→
    40→  <Step title="Explore and Read">
    41→    Use `nia_explore` and `nia_read` to browse dataset structure:
    42→    ```
    43→    "Show me the structure of the indexed gsm8k dataset"
    44→    "Read rows from the train split of squad"
    45→    ```
    46→  </Step>
    47→</Steps>
    48→
    49→## Supported URL Formats
    50→
    51→The `index` tool auto-detects HuggingFace dataset URLs:
    52→
    53→```
    54→# Standard dataset URLs
    55→https://huggingface.co/datasets/squad
    56→https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k
    57→
    58→# Dataset viewer URLs (also supported)
    59→https://huggingface.co/datasets/rajpurkar/squad/viewer
    60→```
    61→
    62→You can also specify the resource type explicitly:
    63→```
    64→index(url="https://huggingface.co/datasets/squad", resource_type="huggingface_dataset")
    65→```
    66→
    67→## Indexing Strategy
    68→
    69→Nia uses intelligent sampling based on dataset size to balance coverage and performance:
    70→
    71→| Dataset Size | Strategy | Rows Indexed |
    72→|--------------|----------|--------------|
    73→| < 200K rows | Full | All rows |
    74→| 200K - 2M rows | Sampled | Up to 100K rows |
    75→| > 2M rows | Sampled | Up to 25K rows |
    76→
    77→<Note>
    78→Binary columns (images, audio, arrays) are automatically excluded. Only text-compatible columns (strings, numbers, booleans) are indexed for semantic search.
    79→</Note>
    80→
    81→## What Gets Indexed
    82→
    83→For each dataset, Nia extracts and indexes:
    84→
    85→- **Row content**: Text from all compatible columns, formatted and chunked for semantic retrieval
    86→- **Dataset metadata**: Schema, splits, column types, row counts, license info
    87→- **Configuration info**: Available configs and the selected configuration
    88→
    89→## MCP Tools
    90→
    91→### Indexing
    92→
    93→Use the unified `index` tool:
    94→
    95→```python
    96→# Via MCP tool
    97→index(url="https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k")
    98→
    99→# Or via API
   100→POST /v2/huggingface-datasets
   101→{
   102→  "url": "https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k"
   103→}
   104→```
   105→
   106→### Searching
   107→
   108→Use `search` with the dataset as a data source:
   109→
   110→```python
   111→search(
   112→    query="math problems involving percentages",
   113→    data_sources=["openai/gsm8k"]  # or use the source UUID
   114→)
   115→```
   116→
   117→### Reading
   118→
   119→Use `nia_read` to read dataset content:
   120→
   121→```python
   122→nia_read(
   123→    source_type="huggingface_dataset",
   124→    doc_source_id="openai/gsm8k",  # dataset ID or source UUID
   125→    path="/"  # optional path filter
   126→)
   127→```
   128→
   129→### Grep (Regex Search)
   130→
   131→Use `nia_grep` to search with regex patterns:
   132→
   133→```python
   134→nia_grep(
   135→    source_type="huggingface_dataset",
   136→    doc_source_id="openai/gsm8k",
   137→    pattern="\\d+%"  # find percentage values
   138→)
   139→```
   140→
   141→### Exploring
   142→
   143→Use `nia_explore` to see dataset structure:
   144→
   145→```python
   146→nia_explore(
   147→    source_type="huggingface_dataset",
   148→    doc_source_id="openai/gsm8k",
   149→    action="tree"
   150→)
   151→```
   152→
   153→## Managing Datasets
   154→
   155→### List Indexed Datasets
   156→
   157→```python
   158→manage_resource(action="list", resource_type="huggingface_dataset")
   159→```
   160→
   161→### Check Status
   162→
   163→```python
   164→manage_resource(
   165→    action="status",
   166→    resource_type="huggingface_dataset",
   167→    identifier="openai/gsm8k"
   168→)
   169→```
   170→
   171→### Delete a Dataset
   172→
   173→```python
   174→manage_resource(
   175→    action="delete",
   176→    resource_type="huggingface_dataset",
   177→    identifier="openai/gsm8k"
   178→)
   179→```
   180→
   181→## Global Source Deduplication
   182→
   183→HuggingFace datasets participate in Nia's global source pool:
   184→
   185→- If someone has already indexed `openai/gsm8k`, you can subscribe instantly without re-indexing
   186→- Use `manage_resource(action="subscribe", identifier="https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k")` to subscribe to an existing index
   187→- Set `add_as_global_source=False` when indexing to keep datasets private
   188→
   189→## API Endpoints
   190→
   191→### Index a Dataset
   192→
   193→```bash
   194→POST /v2/huggingface-datasets
   195→Authorization: Bearer nk_xxx
   196→
   197→{
   198→  "url": "https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gsm8k",
   199→  "config": "main",  # optional dataset config
   200→  "add_as_global_source": true
   201→}
   202→```
   203→
   204→### Response
   205→
   206→```json
   207→{
   208→  "id": "source-uuid",
   209→  "status": "indexing",
   210→  "dataset_id": "gsm8k",
   211→  "metadata": {
   212→    "owner": "openai",
   213→    "description": "Grade school math problems...",
   214→    "splits": ["train", "test"],
   215→    "columns": [
   216→      {"name": "question", "type": "string"},
   217→      {"name": "answer", "type": "string"}
   218→    ],
   219→    "row_count": 8792,
   220→    "sampling_strategy": "full"
   221→  }
   222→}
   223→```
   224→
   225→## Use Cases
   226→
   227→<CardGroup cols={2}>
   228→  <Card title="Fine-tuning Data Discovery" icon="database">
   229→    Search through datasets to find relevant training examples for your specific use case.
   230→  </Card>
   231→
   232→  <Card title="Benchmark Analysis" icon="chart-line">
   233→    Query benchmark datasets to understand evaluation metrics and test case distribution.
   234→  </Card>
   235→
   236→  <Card title="Data Augmentation" icon="layer-group">
   237→    Find similar examples across multiple datasets to augment your training data.
   238→  </Card>
   239→
   240→  <Card title="Dataset Documentation" icon="book">
   241→    Let your agents understand dataset schemas and find specific examples on demand.
   242→  </Card>
   243→</CardGroup>
   244→
   245→## Limitations
   246→
   247→- **Binary data**: Images, audio, and array columns are excluded from indexing
   248→- **Large datasets**: Very large datasets (>2M rows) are sampled to maintain performance
   249→- **Streaming**: Datasets must support HuggingFace's streaming mode
   250→- **Private datasets**: Requires `HF_TOKEN` environment variable for private dataset access
   251→
   252→

1→---
     2→title: 'Slack Search'
     3→description: 'Connect, index, and search Slack workspaces through Nia'
     4→---
     5→
     6→Nia's Slack Search lets you connect Slack workspaces, index message history, and run semantic or keyword searches across all your conversations. It supports two connection models:
     7→
     8→1. **Direct OAuth** -- Connect your own Slack workspace via the Nia dashboard
     9→2. **BYOT (Bring Your Own Token)** -- Register any Slack bot token via the API, designed for enterprise customers who need to index their end-users' workspaces
    10→
    11→All indexed data is stored in isolated, org-scoped namespaces. Real-time message events keep the index fresh after the initial backfill.
    12→
    13→<Info>
    14→  After the initial full index, new messages are indexed automatically in real-time via Slack's Events API. Sends, edits, and deletes in indexed channels are reflected within seconds.
    15→</Info>
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Getting Started
    20→
    21→<Steps>
    22→  <Step title="Connect a Workspace">
    23→    Connect via OAuth from the Nia dashboard, or register a bot token via the API (BYOT).
    24→  </Step>
    25→  <Step title="Configure Channels">
    26→    Choose which channels to index -- all channels, or a specific subset.
    27→  </Step>
    28→  <Step title="Index">
    29→    Trigger a full indexing job. Nia fetches all message history from configured channels, embeds, and stores everything in a vector index.
    30→  </Step>
    31→  <Step title="Search">
    32→    Run semantic or keyword searches across indexed messages via the API or MCP tools.
    33→  </Step>
    34→</Steps>
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## Connecting a Workspace
    39→
    40→### Option 1: OAuth (Dashboard)
    41→
    42→Generate an OAuth URL and redirect the user to authorize:
    43→
    44→```bash
    45→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/install \
    46→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
    47→```
    48→
    49→```json
    50→{
    51→  "url": "https://slack.com/oauth/v2/authorize?client_id=...&scope=...&redirect_uri=..."
    52→}
    53→```
    54→
    55→After authorization, Slack redirects back and the installation is created automatically.
    56→
    57→### Option 2: BYOT (Bring Your Own Token)
    58→
    59→Register an external Slack bot token directly. The workspace owner creates a Slack app, installs it, and provides the bot token to you. No OAuth flow needed.
    60→
    61→```bash
    62→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/install/token \
    63→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
    64→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    65→  -d '{
    66→    "bot_token": "xoxb-...",
    67→    "name": "Customer Workspace"
    68→  }'
    69→```
    70→
    71→```json
    72→{
    73→  "installation_id": "a9e51db8-...",
    74→  "team_id": "T12345",
    75→  "team_name": "Customer Workspace",
    76→  "status": "active"
    77→}
    78→```
    79→
    80→The `installation_id` is used in all subsequent operations.
    81→
    82→---
    83→
    84→## Managing Installations
    85→
    86→### List Installations
    87→
    88→```bash
    89→curl https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations \
    90→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
    91→```
    92→
    93→```json
    94→{
    95→  "installations": [
    96→    {
    97→      "id": "a9e51db8-...",
    98→      "team_id": "T12345",
    99→      "team_name": "Customer Workspace",
   100→      "status": "active",
   101→      "is_external": true,
   102→      "indexed_channel_count": 43,
   103→      "indexed_message_count": 1401,
   104→      "last_sync_at": "2026-02-19T18:05:02Z"
   105→    }
   106→  ]
   107→}
   108→```
   109→
   110→### Configure Channels
   111→
   112→List available channels:
   113→
   114→```bash
   115→curl https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id}/channels \
   116→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   117→```
   118→
   119→Index all channels except specific ones:
   120→
   121→```bash
   122→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id}/channels \
   123→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
   124→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   125→  -d '{
   126→    "mode": "all",
   127→    "exclude_channels": ["C092VB132SE", "C092VB183B4"]
   128→  }'
   129→```
   130→
   131→Or index only specific channels:
   132→
   133→```json
   134→{
   135→  "mode": "selected",
   136→  "include_channels": ["C092TL9QAKF", "C0A15U8B3NW"]
   137→}
   138→```
   139→
   140→### Disconnect
   141→
   142→```bash
   143→curl -X DELETE https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id} \
   144→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   145→```
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→## Indexing
   150→
   151→Trigger a full indexing job that fetches all message history from configured channels:
   152→
   153→```bash
   154→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id}/index \
   155→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   156→```
   157→
   158→```json
   159→{
   160→  "source_id": "3c7ee426-...",
   161→  "installation_id": "a9e51db8-...",
   162→  "status": "processing",
   163→  "workflow_run_id": "be22e100-..."
   164→}
   165→```
   166→
   167→### Check Progress
   168→
   169→```bash
   170→curl https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id}/status \
   171→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   172→```
   173→
   174→```json
   175→{
   176→  "installation_id": "a9e51db8-...",
   177→  "team_name": "Customer Workspace",
   178→  "status": "processing",
   179→  "progress": 45,
   180→  "message": "Indexing #engineering (12/43)",
   181→  "indexed_channel_count": 12,
   182→  "indexed_message_count": 580,
   183→  "chunk_count": 520
   184→}
   185→```
   186→
   187→Indexing speed depends on the Slack app's rate limit tier:
   188→
   189→| Tier | `conversations.history` rate | Who |
   190→|------|------------------------------|-----|
   191→| Tier 1 | ~1 req/min | Non-Marketplace apps |
   192→| Tier 3 | ~50 req/min | Marketplace-listed apps |
   193→
   194→<Note>
   195→  For BYOT, the customer's Slack app rate limits apply. Initial indexing of large workspaces (10k+ messages) may take 30-60 minutes on Tier 1. After the initial index, real-time events keep it current with no rate limit concerns.
   196→</Note>
   197→
   198→---
   199→
   200→## Searching
   201→
   202→### Semantic Search
   203→
   204→Run semantic search across indexed Slack messages with AI synthesis:
   205→
   206→```bash
   207→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/search/query \
   208→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
   209→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   210→  -d '{
   211→    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "what did the team decide about the migration?"}],
   212→    "slack_workspaces": ["a9e51db8-..."],
   213→    "include_sources": true,
   214→    "stream": true
   215→  }'
   216→```
   217→
   218→You can combine Slack with other indexed sources like repositories:
   219→
   220→```json
   221→{
   222→  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "how does our auth system work?"}],
   223→  "repositories": [{"repository": "acme/backend"}],
   224→  "slack_workspaces": ["a9e51db8-..."],
   225→  "stream": true
   226→}
   227→```
   228→
   229→**Streamed response:**
   230→```
   231→data: {"content": "Based on the Slack discussions, the team decided to..."}
   232→data: {"sources": [{"content": "#engineering | @alice (2026-01-15)...", "metadata": {...}}]}
   233→data: [DONE]
   234→```
   235→
   236→### Keyword Search (Grep)
   237→
   238→BM25 full-text keyword search over indexed messages:
   239→
   240→```bash
   241→curl -X POST https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id}/grep \
   242→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY" \
   243→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   244→  -d '{
   245→    "pattern": "migration deadline",
   246→    "channel": "engineering",
   247→    "limit": 20
   248→  }'
   249→```
   250→
   251→```json
   252→{
   253→  "pattern": "migration deadline",
   254→  "channel": "engineering",
   255→  "result_count": 3,
   256→  "results": [
   257→    {
   258→      "text": "#engineering | @alice (2026-01-15 14:30 UTC)\nThe migration deadline is March 1st...",
   259→      "score": 12.5,
   260→      "metadata": {
   261→        "channel_name": "engineering",
   262→        "user_name": "alice",
   263→        "ts": "1736955000.000"
   264→      }
   265→    }
   266→  ]
   267→}
   268→```
   269→
   270→### Read Channel Messages (Live)
   271→
   272→Fetch recent messages directly from the Slack API (live, not from the index):
   273→
   274→```bash
   275→curl "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2/slack/installations/{installation_id}/messages?channel=engineering&limit=50" \
   276→  -H "Authorization: Bearer $NIA_API_KEY"
   277→```
   278→
   279→```json
   280→{
   281→  "channel": "engineering",
   282→  "message_count": 50,
   283→  "messages": [
   284→    "@alice (2026-02-19 14:30 UTC): The deployment went smoothly",
   285→    "@bob (2026-02-19 14:32 UTC): Great, I'll update the status page"
   286→  ]
   287→}
   288→```
   289→
   290→---
   291→
   292→## MCP Integration
   293→
   294→Slack workspaces are searchable via Nia's MCP tools once indexed:
   295→
   296→```python
   297→# Semantic search across Slack
   298→search(query="deployment issues last week", slack_workspaces=["a9e51db8-..."])
   299→
   300→# Keyword grep in Slack
   301→nia_grep(source_type="slack", slack_installation_id="a9e51db8-...", pattern="deadline")
   302→
   303→# Read messages from a channel
   304→nia_read(source_type="slack", slack_installation_id="a9e51db8-...", channel="engineering")
   305→
   306→# Explore channels
   307→nia_explore(source_type="slack", slack_installation_id="a9e51db8-...")
   308→```
   309→
   310→---
   311→
   312→## Enterprise Multi-Tenant Model (BYOT)
   313→
   314→For enterprises managing multiple customer workspaces, BYOT provides a clean multi-tenant architecture:
   315→
   316→```
   317→                ┌─────────────────────────┐
   318→                │    Enterprise (Acme)     │
   319→                │    Nia API Key: nk_...   │
   320→                └────────┬────────────────┘
   321→                         │
   322→          ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
   323→          │              │              │
   324→┌─────────▼──┐  ┌───────▼────┐  ┌──────▼─────┐
   325→│ Customer A │  │ Customer B │  │ Customer C │
   326→│ xoxb-aaa   │  │ xoxb-bbb   │  │ xoxb-ccc   │
   327→│ inst: id-1 │  │ inst: id-2 │  │ inst: id-3 │
   328→└────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘
   329→```
   330→
   331→**How it works:**
   332→
   333→<Steps>
   334→  <Step title="Get an API Key">
   335→    Sign up for Nia and get your API key from [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai).
   336→  </Step>
   337→  <Step title="Collect Bot Tokens">
   338→    Each customer creates a Slack app in their own workspace and shares the bot token with you.
   339→  </Step>
   340→  <Step title="Register Each Token">
   341→    Register each token via `POST /v2/slack/install/token`. Each workspace gets its own isolated namespace.
   342→  </Step>
   343→  <Step title="Search Any Workspace">
   344→    Search any workspace by passing the `installation_id`, or search multiple at once:
   345→    ```json
   346→    {
   347→      "slack_workspaces": ["id-1", "id-2", "id-3"]
   348→    }
   349→    ```
   350→  </Step>
   351→</Steps>
   352→
   353→**Privacy guarantees:**
   354→- Each workspace is stored in a separate vector namespace scoped to your organization
   355→- Customers never interact with Nia directly
   356→- Customers can revoke access by uninstalling their Slack app at any time
   357→- Bot tokens are encrypted at rest (Fernet AES-128-CBC)
   358→
   359→---
   360→
   361→## Required Slack Bot Scopes
   362→
   363→When customers create their Slack app, they need these bot token scopes:
   364→
   365→| Scope | Purpose |
   366→|-------|---------|
   367→| `channels:read` | List public channels |
   368→| `channels:history` | Read public channel messages |
   369→| `channels:join` | Auto-join public channels for indexing |
   370→| `groups:read` | List private channels (optional) |
   371→| `groups:history` | Read private channel messages (optional) |
   372→| `users:read` | Resolve user display names |
   373→| `reactions:read` | Read message reactions |
   374→
   375→<Note>
   376→  **Minimum required:** `channels:read`, `channels:history`, `users:read`. The other scopes are optional but recommended for full coverage.
   377→</Note>
   378→
   379→---
   380→
   381→<Warning>
   382→**Need Help?** Join our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/BBSwUMrrfn) or reach out through [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai/) for support.
   383→</Warning>

1→---
     2→title: 'Installation'
     3→description: 'Install Nia and configure it for your coding agent.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Quick Install
     7→
     8→Run a single command to install Nia:
     9→
    10→```bash
    11→npx nia-wizard@latest        # npm
    12→pnpx nia-wizard@latest       # pnpm
    13→bunx nia-wizard@latest       # bun (fastest)
    14→yarn dlx nia-wizard@latest   # yarn
    15→```
    16→
    17→This wizard will:
    18→- Work for both new and existing users
    19→- Create your account or log you in
    20→- Generate your API key automatically
    21→- Auto-detect and configure your IDE
    22→- Get you running in under a minute
    23→
    24→<Info>
    25→  That's it! The wizard handles everything - just follow the prompts and you're done.
    26→</Info>
    27→
    28→<Accordion title="Alternative installation method">
    29→  ```bash
    30→  curl -fsSL https://app.trynia.ai/cli | sh
    31→  ```
    32→</Accordion>
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## Choose Your Setup
    37→
    38→There are two ways to connect Nia to your coding agent. Both give you access to the same features and tools — they just differ in how they communicate with the Nia API.
    39→
    40→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    41→  <Card title="MCP Server" icon="server" href="/integrations/installation/mcp">
    42→    The standard approach. Your IDE connects to Nia through the **Model Context Protocol** — either via a remote server (recommended) or a local process.
    43→
    44→    - Native IDE support for 30+ editors
    45→    - Automatic context management
    46→    - Remote server option (zero dependencies)
    47→    - Best for complex, multi-step workflows
    48→  </Card>
    49→
    50→  <Card title="Agent Skill" icon="bolt" href="/integrations/installation/skill">
    51→    A lightweight alternative. Your agent reads a skill file and calls the Nia API directly — no MCP server or background process needed.
    52→
    53→    - Single command install
    54→    - No background processes
    55→    - Works with any agent that supports skills
    56→    - Lighter resource usage, sometimes faster than MCP
    57→  </Card>
    58→</CardGroup>
    59→
    60→<Info>
    61→  **Not sure which to pick?** If your IDE natively supports MCP (most do), go with the **MCP Server**. If you prefer a simpler setup or your agent supports skills, try the **Agent Skill** — it uses direct API calls and can be even more responsive.
    62→</Info>
    63→

1→---
     2→title: 'MCP Server'
     3→description: 'Install and configure the Nia MCP server for your coding agent.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Manual Configuration
     7→
     8→<Info>
     9→  **Remote Server** is recommended - no dependencies, no local process. If remote keeps failing (especially in Claude Code), switch to Local Server.
    10→</Info>
    11→
    12→<Note>
    13→  **Local Server requires:** Python 3.8+ and `pipx` (recommended) or `uvx`
    14→</Note>
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Client-Specific Configuration
    19→
    20→Choose your coding agent below for detailed configuration instructions. Each section includes both **Remote Server (Recommended)** and **Local Server** options.
    21→
    22→<Accordion title="Cursor">
    23→  Go to: **Settings → Cursor Settings → MCP → Add new global MCP server**
    24→
    25→  In Cursor 1.0 and later, manually edit `~/.cursor/mcp.json`:
    26→  - **macOS**: `~/.cursor/mcp.json`
    27→  - **Windows**: `%APPDATA%\Cursor\mcp.json`
    28→  - **Linux**: `~/.config/cursor/mcp.json`
    29→
    30→  See [Cursor MCP docs](https://docs.cursor.com/context/model-context-protocol) for more info.
    31→
    32→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
    33→
    34→  ```json
    35→  {
    36→    "mcpServers": {
    37→      "nia": {
    38→        "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp",
    39→        "headers": {
    40→          "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
    41→        }
    42→      }
    43→    }
    44→  }
    45→  ```
    46→
    47→  **Local Server:**
    48→
    49→  ```json
    50→  {
    51→    "mcpServers": {
    52→      "nia": {
    53→        "command": "pipx",
    54→        "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
    55→        "env": {
    56→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
    57→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
    58→        }
    59→      }
    60→    }
    61→  }
    62→  ```
    63→</Accordion>
    64→
    65→<Accordion title="VS Code">
    66→  Open your VS Code MCP configuration:
    67→  - Run **MCP: Open User Configuration** from the Command Palette
    68→  - Or manually edit:
    69→    - **macOS/Linux**: `~/.config/Code/User/mcp.json`
    70→    - **Windows**: `%APPDATA%\Code\User\mcp.json`
    71→
    72→  See [VS Code MCP docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/chat/mcp-servers) for more info.
    73→
    74→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
    75→
    76→  ```json
    77→  "mcp": {
    78→    "servers": {
    79→      "nia": {
    80→        "type": "http",
    81→        "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp",
    82→        "headers": {
    83→          "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
    84→        }
    85→      }
    86→    }
    87→  }
    88→  ```
    89→
    90→  **Local Server:**
    91→
    92→  ```json
    93→  "mcp": {
    94→    "servers": {
    95→      "nia": {
    96→        "type": "stdio",
    97→        "command": "pipx",
    98→        "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
    99→        "env": {
   100→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
   101→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
   102→        }
   103→      }
   104→    }
   105→  }
   106→  ```
   107→</Accordion>
   108→
   109→<Accordion title="Claude Code">
   110→  See [Claude Code MCP docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/mcp) for more info.
   111→
   112→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
   113→
   114→  ```bash
   115→  # For current project only (default)
   116→  claude mcp add --transport http --header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" nia "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp"
   117→
   118→  # For global installation (all projects)
   119→  claude mcp add --transport http --header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" --scope user nia "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp"
   120→  ```
   121→
   122→  **Local Server:**
   123→
   124→  ```bash
   125→  # For current project only (default)
   126→  claude mcp add -e NIA_API_KEY=YOUR_API_KEY -e NIA_API_URL=https://apigcp.trynia.ai/ nia -- pipx run --no-cache nia-mcp-server
   127→
   128→  # For global installation (all projects)
   129→  claude mcp add --scope user -e NIA_API_KEY=YOUR_API_KEY -e NIA_API_URL=https://apigcp.trynia.ai/ nia -- pipx run --no-cache nia-mcp-server
   130→  ```
   131→
   132→  **Scope Options:**
   133→  | Scope | Description |
   134→  |-------|-------------|
   135→  | `--scope local` | Current project only (default) - stored in `.mcp.json` |
   136→  | `--scope user` | All projects for current user - stored in `~/.claude/settings.json` |
   137→
   138→  <Note>
   139→    Use `--scope user` if you want Nia available across all your projects without configuring each one separately.
   140→  </Note>
   141→</Accordion>
   142→

   523→<Accordion title="Kilo Code">
   524→  You can configure the Nia MCP server in **Kilo Code** using either the UI or by editing your project's MCP configuration file.
   525→
   526→  Kilo Code supports two configuration levels:
   527→  - **Global MCP Configuration** — stored in `mcp_settings.json`
   528→  - **Project-level MCP Configuration** — stored in `.kilocode/mcp.json` (recommended)
   529→
   530→  ### Configure via Kilo Code UI
   531→
   532→  1. Open **Kilo Code**
   533→  2. Click the **Settings** icon in the top-right corner
   534→  3. Navigate to **Settings → MCP Servers**
   535→  4. Click **Add Server**
   536→  5. Choose **HTTP Server** (Streamable HTTP Transport)
   537→  6. Enter the details:
   538→     - **URL**: `https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp`
   539→     - **Headers → Add Header**
   540→       - **Key:** `Authorization`
   541→       - **Value:** `Bearer YOUR_API_KEY`
   542→  7. Click **Save**
   543→
   544→  ### Manual Configuration
   545→
   546→  Create `.kilocode/mcp.json`:
   547→
   548→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
   549→
   550→  ```json
   551→  {
   552→    "mcpServers": {
   553→      "nia": {
   554→        "type": "streamable-http",
   555→        "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp",
   556→        "headers": {
   557→          "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
   558→        },
   559→        "alwaysAllow": [],
   560→        "disabled": false
   561→      }
   562→    }
   563→  }
   564→  ```
   565→
   566→  **Local Server:**
   567→
   568→  ```json
   569→  {
   570→    "mcpServers": {
   571→      "nia": {
   572→        "command": "pipx",
   573→        "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
   574→        "env": {
   575→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
   576→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
   577→        },
   578→        "alwaysAllow": [],
   579→        "disabled": false
   580→      }
   581→    }
   582→  }
   583→  ```
   584→</Accordion>
   585→

   950→          "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
   951→        },
   952→        "enabled": true
   953→      }
   954→    }
   955→  }
   956→  ```
   957→
   958→  **Local Server:**
   959→
   960→  ```json
   961→  {
   962→    "mcp": {
   963→      "nia": {
   964→        "type": "local",
   965→        "command": ["pipx", "run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
   966→        "environment": {
   967→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
   968→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
   969→        },
   970→        "enabled": true
   971→      }
   972→    }
   973→  }
   974→  ```
   975→</Accordion>
   976→
   977→<Accordion title="Crush">
   978→  Add this to your Crush configuration file. See [Crush MCP docs](https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush#mcps) for more info.
   979→
   980→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
   981→
   982→  ```json
   983→  {
   984→    "$schema": "https://charm.land/crush.json",
   985→    "mcp": {
   986→      "nia": {
   987→        "type": "http",
   988→        "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp",
   989→        "headers": {
   990→          "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
   991→        }
   992→      }
   993→    }
   994→  }
   995→  ```
   996→
   997→  **Local Server:**
   998→
   999→  ```json
  1000→  {
  1001→    "$schema": "https://charm.land/crush.json",
  1002→    "mcp": {
  1003→      "nia": {
  1004→        "type": "stdio",
  1005→        "command": "pipx",
  1006→        "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
  1007→        "env": {
  1008→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1009→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1010→        }
  1011→      }
  1012→    }
  1013→  }
  1014→  ```
  1015→</Accordion>
  1016→
  1017→<Accordion title="Amp">
  1018→  See [Amp MCP docs](https://ampcode.com/manual#mcp) for more info.
  1019→
  1020→  **Remote Server Only:**
  1021→
  1022→  ```bash
  1023→  amp mcp add nia --header "Authorization=Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp
  1024→  ```
  1025→</Accordion>
  1026→
  1027→<Accordion title="Factory">
  1028→  Factory's droid supports MCP servers through its CLI. See [Factory MCP docs](https://docs.factory.ai/cli/configuration/mcp) for more info.
  1029→
  1030→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
  1031→
  1032→  ```bash
  1033→  droid mcp add nia https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp --type http --header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
  1034→  ```
  1035→
  1036→  **Local Server:**
  1037→
  1038→  ```bash
  1039→  droid mcp add nia "pipx run --no-cache nia-mcp-server" --env NIA_API_KEY=YOUR_API_KEY --env NIA_API_URL=https://apigcp.trynia.ai/
  1040→  ```
  1041→
  1042→  Once configured, Nia tools will be available in your droid sessions. Type `/mcp` within droid to manage servers.
  1043→</Accordion>
  1044→
  1045→<Accordion title="Augment Code">
  1046→  Via UI:
  1047→  1. Hamburger menu → Settings → Tools
  1048→  2. Click **+ Add MCP**
  1049→  3. Enter command: `pipx run --no-cache nia-mcp-server`
  1050→  4. Name: `Nia`
  1051→
  1052→  **Manual Configuration:**
  1053→
  1054→  ```json
  1055→  "augment.advanced": {
  1056→    "mcpServers": [
  1057→      {
  1058→        "name": "nia",
  1059→        "command": "pipx",
  1060→        "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
  1061→        "env": {
  1062→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1063→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1064→        }
  1065→      }
  1066→    ]
  1067→  }
  1068→  ```
  1069→</Accordion>
  1070→
  1071→<Accordion title="Rovo Dev CLI">
  1072→  Edit your Rovo Dev CLI MCP config:
  1073→
  1074→  ```bash
  1075→  acli rovodev mcp
  1076→  ```
  1077→
  1078→  **Remote Server (Recommended):**
  1079→
  1080→  ```json
  1081→  {
  1082→    "mcpServers": {
  1083→      "nia": {
  1084→        "url": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/mcp",
  1085→        "headers": {
  1086→          "Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
  1087→        }
  1088→      }
  1089→    }
  1090→  }
  1091→  ```
  1092→
  1093→  **Local Server:**
  1094→
  1095→  ```json
  1096→  {
  1097→    "mcpServers": {
  1098→      "nia": {
  1099→        "command": "pipx",
  1100→        "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
  1101→        "env": {
  1102→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1103→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1104→        }
  1105→      }
  1106→    }
  1107→  }
  1108→  ```
  1109→</Accordion>
  1110→
  1111→<Accordion title="Smithery">
  1112→  To install Nia MCP Server for any client automatically via [Smithery](https://smithery.ai):
  1113→
  1114→  ```bash
  1115→  npx -y @smithery/cli@latest install nia-mcp-server --client <CLIENT_NAME> --key <YOUR_SMITHERY_KEY>
  1116→  ```
  1117→
  1118→  You can find your Smithery key in the [Smithery.ai webpage](https://smithery.ai).
  1119→</Accordion>
  1120→
  1121→<Accordion title="Zencoder">
  1122→  1. Go to the Zencoder menu (...)
  1123→  2. Select **Agent tools**
  1124→  3. Click on **Add custom MCP**
  1125→  4. Add the name and configuration:
  1126→
  1127→  ```json
  1128→  {
  1129→    "command": "pipx",
  1130→    "args": ["run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
  1131→    "env": {
  1132→      "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1133→      "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1134→    }
  1135→  }
  1136→  ```
  1137→
  1138→  5. Click **Install**
  1139→</Accordion>
  1140→
  1141→<Accordion title="Emdash">
  1142→  [Emdash](https://github.com/generalaction/emdash) is an orchestration layer for running multiple coding agents in parallel.
  1143→
  1144→  **What Emdash provides:**
  1145→  - Global toggle: Settings → MCP → "Enable Nia MCP"
  1146→  - Per-workspace enable: The Nia button in the ProviderBar
  1147→
  1148→  **What you still need to do:**
  1149→  Configure your coding agent (Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) to connect to Nia MCP. Emdash does not modify your agent's config.
  1150→
  1151→  See the [Emdash repository](https://github.com/generalaction/emdash) for more information.
  1152→</Accordion>
  1153→
  1154→<Accordion title="Using Bun or Deno">
  1155→  Use these alternatives to run the local Nia MCP server with other runtimes.
  1156→
  1157→  **Bun:**
  1158→
  1159→  ```json
  1160→  {
  1161→    "mcpServers": {
  1162→      "nia": {
  1163→        "command": "bunx",
  1164→        "args": ["nia-mcp-server"],
  1165→        "env": {
  1166→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1167→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1168→        }
  1169→      }
  1170→    }
  1171→  }
  1172→  ```
  1173→
  1174→  **Deno:**
  1175→
  1176→  ```json
  1177→  {
  1178→    "mcpServers": {
  1179→      "nia": {
  1180→        "command": "deno",
  1181→        "args": [
  1182→          "run",
  1183→          "--allow-env",
  1184→          "--allow-net",
  1185→          "npm:nia-mcp-server"
  1186→        ],
  1187→        "env": {
  1188→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1189→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1190→        }
  1191→      }
  1192→    }
  1193→  }
  1194→  ```
  1195→</Accordion>
  1196→
  1197→<Accordion title="Using Docker">
  1198→  1. Create a `Dockerfile`:
  1199→
  1200→  ```dockerfile
  1201→  FROM python:3.11-slim
  1202→  WORKDIR /app
  1203→  RUN pip install nia-mcp-server
  1204→  CMD ["nia-mcp-server"]
  1205→  ```
  1206→
  1207→  2. Build the image:
  1208→
  1209→  ```bash
  1210→  docker build -t nia-mcp .
  1211→  ```
  1212→
  1213→  3. Configure your MCP client:
  1214→
  1215→  ```json
  1216→  {
  1217→    "mcpServers": {
  1218→      "nia": {
  1219→        "command": "docker",
  1220→        "args": ["run", "-i", "--rm", "-e", "NIA_API_KEY=YOUR_API_KEY", "-e", "NIA_API_URL=https://apigcp.trynia.ai/", "nia-mcp"],
  1221→        "transportType": "stdio"
  1222→      }
  1223→    }
  1224→  }
  1225→  ```
  1226→</Accordion>
  1227→
  1228→<Accordion title="Windows Configuration">
  1229→  On Windows, configuration differs slightly. Use `cmd` with `/c`:
  1230→
  1231→  ```json
  1232→  {
  1233→    "mcpServers": {
  1234→      "nia": {
  1235→        "command": "cmd",
  1236→        "args": ["/c", "pipx", "run", "--no-cache", "nia-mcp-server"],
  1237→        "env": {
  1238→          "NIA_API_KEY": "YOUR_API_KEY",
  1239→          "NIA_API_URL": "https://apigcp.trynia.ai/"
  1240→        },
  1241→        "disabled": false,
  1242→        "autoApprove": []
  1243→      }
  1244→    }
  1245→  }
  1246→  ```
  1247→</Accordion>
  1248→
  1249→---

  1252→**Need Help?** Join our community or reach out through [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai/) for support and updates.
  1253→</Warning>

1→---
     2→title: 'Agent Skill'
     3→description: 'Use Nia without MCP server — your agent calls API endpoints directly through a skill.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→The Nia Agent Skill allows you to use Nia without running an MCP server. Instead, your agent calls the Nia API endpoints directly through the skill file, giving you a lightweight alternative to the full MCP setup.
     7→
     8→<Info>
     9→  **No Server Required**: The skill approach doesn't require running any background process—your agent reads the skill file and makes API calls directly.
    10→</Info>
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## How It Works
    15→
    16→When you install the Nia skill, it provides your agent with:
    17→
    18→- **API endpoint definitions** for all Nia features
    19→- **Authentication instructions** for using your API key
    20→- **Usage examples** the agent can follow
    21→
    22→Your agent reads the skill file and uses it as a reference to make HTTP requests directly to the Nia API, bypassing the need for an MCP server.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Installation
    27→
    28→Install the Nia skill in non-interactive mode (recommended for agents/automation):
    29→
    30→```bash
    31→npx nia-wizard skill add \
    32→  --api-key "nk_your_api_key" \
    33→  --source nozomio-labs/nia-skill \
    34→  --non-interactive \
    35→  --ci
    36→```
    37→
    38→Optional target pinning:
    39→
    40→```bash
    41→npx nia-wizard skill add \
    42→  --api-key "nk_your_api_key" \
    43→  --target codex \
    44→  --non-interactive \
    45→  --ci
    46→```
    47→
    48→Manual installation via skills CLI:
    49→
    50→```bash
    51→npx skills add nozomio-labs/nia-skill        # npm
    52→pnpx skills add nozomio-labs/nia-skill       # pnpm
    53→bunx skills add nozomio-labs/nia-skill       # bun (fastest)
    54→yarn dlx skills add nozomio-labs/nia-skill   # yarn
    55→```
    56→
    57→---
    58→
    59→## Setup
    60→
    61→### 1. Get Your API Key
    62→
    63→Sign up at [trynia.ai](https://trynia.ai) to get your API key.
    64→
    65→For full headless onboarding (API signup/login + non-interactive install), use the [Agent Onboarding guide](/agent-onboarding).
    66→
    67→### 2. Configure Your Agent
    68→
    69→The skill will instruct your agent how to authenticate. Typically, you'll set your API key in one of these ways:
    70→
    71→- Environment variable: `NIA_API_KEY`
    72→- Config file: `~/.config/nia/api_key`
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Links
    77→
    78→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    79→  <Card title="GitHub" icon="github" href="https://github.com/nozomio-labs/nia-skill">
    80→    View skill source code
    81→  </Card>
    82→
    83→  <Card title="Get API Key" icon="key" href="https://app.trynia.ai">
    84→    Sign up for Nia API access
    85→  </Card>
    86→</CardGroup>
    87→
    88→---
    89→
    90→<Warning>
    91→**Need Help?** Join our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/BBSwUMrrfn) for support.
    92→</Warning>
    93→

1→---
     2→title: 'Claude Code Plugin'
     3→description: 'Index and search code repositories, documentation, research papers, and HuggingFace datasets with Nia AI in Claude Code.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→The Nia plugin for [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) provides direct access to Nia for indexing and searching code repositories, documentation, research papers, and HuggingFace datasets.
     7→
     8→<Info>
     9→  **Marketplace Install**: Install directly from the Claude Code marketplace with a single command.
    10→</Info>
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Features
    15→
    16→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    17→  <Card title="Repository Indexing" icon="code-branch">
    18→    Index GitHub repositories and search through code with semantic and regex search.
    19→  </Card>
    20→
    21→  <Card title="Documentation Search" icon="book">
    22→    Index entire documentation sites and search across all pages.
    23→  </Card>
    24→
    25→  <Card title="Research Papers" icon="file-lines">
    26→    Index arXiv papers by ID, URL, or PDF link for AI-powered research.
    27→  </Card>
    28→
    29→  <Card title="HuggingFace Datasets" icon="database">
    30→    Index and search HuggingFace datasets by name or URL.
    31→  </Card>
    32→</CardGroup>
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## Installation
    37→
    38→### From Marketplace
    39→
    40→```bash
    41→/install nia
    42→```
    43→
    44→### From GitHub
    45→
    46→```bash
    47→/plugin marketplace add nozomio-labs/nia-plugin
    48→/plugin install nia@nozomio-labs/nia-plugin
    49→```
    50→
    51→---
    52→
    53→## Setup
    54→
    55→### 1. Get Your API Key
    56→
    57→Either run the guided wizard:
    58→
    59→```bash
    60→npx nia-wizard@latest
    61→```
    62→
    63→Or sign up at [trynia.ai](https://trynia.ai) to get your key.
    64→
    65→### 2. Store the Key
    66→
    67→```bash
    68→mkdir -p ~/.config/nia
    69→echo "your-api-key" > ~/.config/nia/api_key
    70→```
    71→
    72→### 3. Requirements
    73→
    74→- `curl`
    75→- `jq`
    76→
    77→<Note>
    78→  Most systems have these pre-installed. On macOS, install with `brew install jq` if needed.
    79→</Note>
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## Available Scripts
    84→
    85→| Script | Description |
    86→|--------|-------------|
    87→| `repos-list.sh` | List indexed repositories |
    88→| `repos-index.sh` | Index a GitHub repository |
    89→| `repos-tree.sh` | Get repository file tree |
    90→| `repos-read.sh` | Read file from repository |
    91→| `repos-grep.sh` | Search code in repository |
    92→| `sources-list.sh` | List indexed data sources |
    93→| `sources-index.sh` | Index documentation URL |
    94→| `sources-tree.sh` | Get source tree |
    95→| `sources-read.sh` | Read from source |
    96→| `papers-list.sh` | List indexed papers |
    97→| `papers-index.sh` | Index arXiv paper |
    98→| `datasets-list.sh` | List HuggingFace datasets |
    99→| `datasets-index.sh` | Index HuggingFace dataset |
   100→| `search-universal.sh` | Search all indexed sources |
   101→| `search-web.sh` | Web search |
   102→| `search-deep.sh` | Deep research (Pro) |
   103→| `package-grep.sh` | Search package source code |
   104→| `global-subscribe.sh` | Subscribe to public source |
   105→| `oracle.sh` | Run autonomous research (Pro) |
   106→
   107→---
   108→
   109→## Usage Examples
   110→
   111→```bash
   112→# List indexed repositories
   113→./scripts/repos-list.sh
   114→
   115→# Index a repository
   116→./scripts/repos-index.sh "owner/repo"
   117→
   118→# Search all indexed sources
   119→./scripts/search-universal.sh "how does auth work?"
   120→
   121→# Index documentation
   122→./scripts/sources-index.sh "https://docs.stripe.com"
   123→
   124→# Grep repository code
   125→./scripts/repos-grep.sh "vercel/ai" "streamText"
   126→```
   127→

   137→  <Card title="Get API Key" icon="key" href="https://app.trynia.ai">
   138→    Sign up for Nia API access
   139→  </Card>
   140→</CardGroup>

1→---
     2→title: 'OpenCode Plugin'
     3→description: 'Integrate Nia knowledge tools directly into OpenCode with automatic research triggers and context sharing.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→The Nia OpenCode plugin integrates Nia's knowledge tools directly into [OpenCode](https://opencode.ai), providing automatic research triggers, context saving for cross-agent handoffs, and full MCP tool access.
     7→
     8→<Info>
     9→  **Automatic Detection**: The plugin detects when you need docs or research and prompts the agent to use Nia tools automatically.
    10→</Info>
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Demo
    15→
    16→<Frame>
    17→  <iframe
    18→    width="100%"
    19→    height="400"
    20→    src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/dtKlVQ_FgGA"
    21→    title="Nia OpenCode Plugin Demo"
    22→    frameBorder="0"
    23→    allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture"
    24→    allowFullScreen
    25→  />
    26→</Frame>
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## Features
    31→
    32→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    33→  <Card title="Research Triggers" icon="magnifying-glass">
    34→    Automatically detects when you need documentation or research and prompts the agent to use Nia.
    35→  </Card>
    36→
    37→  <Card title="Save for Later" icon="floppy-disk">
    38→    Detects when you want to save context for cross-agent handoffs between sessions.
    39→  </Card>
    40→
    41→  <Card title="AGENTS.md Setup" icon="file-code">
    42→    Automatically adds Nia workflow instructions to your project's agent configuration.
    43→  </Card>
    44→
    45→  <Card title="MCP Integration" icon="plug">
    46→    Connects to Nia's MCP server for full tool access including search, grep, and explore.
    47→  </Card>
    48→</CardGroup>
    49→
    50→---
    51→
    52→## Installation
    53→
    54→Install the plugin using `bunx`:
    55→
    56→```bash
    57→bunx nia-opencode@latest install
    58→```
    59→
    60→The installer will prompt for your API key and configure everything automatically.
    61→
    62→### Non-Interactive Installation
    63→
    64→For automated setups or CI environments:
    65→
    66→```bash
    67→bunx nia-opencode@latest install --no-tui --api-key nk_xxx
    68→```
    69→
    70→<Note>
    71→  Get your API key from [app.trynia.ai](https://app.trynia.ai).
    72→</Note>
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Keyword Triggers
    77→
    78→The plugin listens for specific keywords in your conversations to automatically trigger Nia tools.
    79→
    80→### Research Triggers
    81→
    82→Use these phrases to trigger documentation and code research:
    83→
    84→| Phrase | Action |
    85→|--------|--------|
    86→| `research...` | Initiates Nia research |
    87→| `look up...` | Searches documentation |
    88→| `find docs...` | Finds relevant documentation |
    89→| `search codebase...` | Searches indexed repositories |
    90→| `grep for...` | Runs pattern search |
    91→| `index this repo` | Indexes current repository |
    92→
    93→### Save Triggers
    94→
    95→Use these phrases to save context for later or hand off to other agents:
    96→
    97→| Phrase | Action |
    98→|--------|--------|
    99→| `save this context` | Saves current session context |
   100→| `continue later` | Persists work for future sessions |
   101→| `hand off to Cursor` | Prepares context for Cursor handoff |
   102→| `pick this up tomorrow` | Saves context for later continuation |
   103→
   104→---
   105→
   106→## Available Tools
   107→
   108→Once installed, the following Nia tools are available in OpenCode:
   109→
   110→| Tool | Description |
   111→|------|-------------|
   112→| `nia.search` | Semantic search across repositories and documentation |
   113→| `nia.nia_research` | AI-powered research with quick, deep, and oracle modes |
   114→| `nia.index` | Index repositories, documentation, or local folders |
   115→| `nia.nia_read` | Read files from indexed sources |
   116→| `nia.nia_grep` | Regex search in indexed code |

1→---
     2→title: 'ClawHub Skill'
     3→description: 'Index and search code repositories, documentation, research papers, and HuggingFace datasets with Nia AI in Clawdbot.'
     4→---
     5→
     6→The Nia skill for [Clawdbot](https://clawhub.ai) provides direct API access to Nia for indexing and searching code repositories, documentation, research papers, and HuggingFace datasets.
     7→
     8→<Info>
     9→  **Shell-Based Integration**: Uses lightweight shell scripts with `curl` and `jq` for maximum compatibility and portability.
    10→</Info>
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Features
    15→
    16→<CardGroup cols={2}>
    17→  <Card title="Repository Indexing" icon="code-branch">
    18→    Index GitHub repositories and search through code with semantic and regex search.
    19→  </Card>
    20→
    21→  <Card title="Documentation Search" icon="book">
    22→    Index entire documentation sites and search across all pages.
    23→  </Card>
    24→
    25→  <Card title="Research Papers" icon="file-lines">
    26→    Index arXiv papers by ID, URL, or PDF link for AI-powered research.
    27→  </Card>
    28→
    29→  <Card title="HuggingFace Datasets" icon="database">
    30→    Index and search HuggingFace datasets by name or URL.
    31→  </Card>
    32→</CardGroup>
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## Installation
    37→
    38→### 1. Get Your API Key
    39→
    40→Either run the guided wizard:
    41→
    42→```bash
    43→npx nia-wizard@latest
    44→```
    45→
    46→Or sign up at [trynia.ai](https://trynia.ai) to get your key.
    47→
    48→### 2. Store the Key
    49→
    50→```bash
    51→mkdir -p ~/.config/nia
    52→echo "your-api-key-here" > ~/.config/nia/api_key
    53→```
    54→
    55→### 3. Requirements
    56→
    57→- `curl`
    58→- `jq`
    59→
    60→<Note>
    61→  Most systems have these pre-installed. On macOS, install with `brew install jq` if needed.
    62→</Note>
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## Nia-First Workflow
    67→
    68→Before using web fetch or web search, you should:
    69→
    70→1. **Check indexed sources first** using `repos-list.sh` or `sources-list.sh`
    71→2. **If source exists**: Use `search-universal.sh`, `repos-grep.sh`, `sources-read.sh` for targeted queries
    72→3. **If source doesn't exist but you know the URL**: Index it with `repos-index.sh` or `sources-index.sh`, then search
    73→4. **Only if source unknown**: Use `search-web.sh` or `search-deep.sh` to discover URLs, then index
    74→
    75→<Info>
    76→  **Why this matters**: Indexed sources provide more accurate, complete context than web fetches. Web fetch returns truncated/summarized content while Nia provides full source code and documentation.
    77→</Info>
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## Available Scripts
    82→
    83→All scripts are in `./scripts/`. Base URL: `https://apigcp.trynia.ai/v2`
    84→
    85→### Repositories
    86→
    87→| Script | Description |
    88→|--------|-------------|
    89→| `repos-list.sh` | List indexed repos |
    90→| `repos-index.sh "owner/repo" [branch]` | Index a repo |
    91→| `repos-status.sh "owner/repo"` | Get repo status |
    92→| `repos-tree.sh "owner/repo" [branch]` | Get repo tree |
    93→| `repos-read.sh "owner/repo" "path/to/file"` | Read file |
    94→| `repos-grep.sh "owner/repo" "pattern"` | Grep code |
    95→
    96→### Data Sources (Docs, Papers, Datasets)
    97→
    98→| Script | Description |
    99→|--------|-------------|
   100→| `sources-list.sh [type]` | List sources (documentation, research_paper, huggingface_dataset) |
   101→| `sources-index.sh "https://docs.example.com"` | Index docs |
   102→| `sources-tree.sh "source_id_or_name"` | Get source tree |
   103→| `sources-read.sh "source_id" "/path"` | Read from source |
   104→| `sources-grep.sh "source_id" "pattern"` | Grep content |
   105→
   106→### Research Papers (arXiv)
   107→
   108→| Script | Description |
   109→|--------|-------------|
   110→| `papers-list.sh` | List indexed papers |
   111→| `papers-index.sh "2312.00752"` | Index paper (ID, URL, or PDF URL) |
   112→
   113→### HuggingFace Datasets
   114→
   115→| Script | Description |
   116→|--------|-------------|
   117→| `datasets-list.sh` | List indexed datasets |
   118→| `datasets-index.sh "squad"` | Index dataset (name, owner/dataset, or URL) |
   119→
   120→### Search
   121→
   122→| Script | Description |
   123→|--------|-------------|
   124→| `search-universal.sh "query"` | Search ALL indexed sources |
   125→| `search-web.sh "query" [num_results]` | Web search |
   126→| `search-deep.sh "query"` | Deep research (Pro) |
   127→
   128→### Package Search
   129→
   130→| Script | Description |
   131→|--------|-------------|
   132→| `package-grep.sh "npm" "react" "pattern"` | Grep package (npm, py_pi, crates_io, golang_proxy) |
   133→| `package-hybrid.sh "npm" "react" "query"` | Semantic search in packages |
   134→
   135→### Oracle Research (Pro)
   136→
   137→| Script | Description |
   138→|--------|-------------|
   139→| `oracle.sh "research query"` | Run autonomous research |
   140→| `oracle-sessions.sh` | List research sessions |
   141→
   142→---
   143→
   144→## API Reference
   145→
   146→| Type | Endpoint | Identifier Examples |
   147→|------|----------|---------------------|
   148→| Repository | `POST /repositories` | `owner/repo`, `microsoft/vscode` |
   149→| Documentation | `POST /data-sources` | `https://docs.example.com` |
   150→| Research Paper | `POST /research-papers` | `2312.00752`, arXiv URL |
   151→| HuggingFace Dataset | `POST /huggingface-datasets` | `squad`, `owner/dataset` |
   152→
   153→---

   160→  </Card>
   161→
   162→  <Card title="Get API Key" icon="key" href="https://app.trynia.ai">
   163→    Sign up for Nia API access
   164→  </Card>
   165→</CardGroup>

     5→
     6→This section contains practical examples and workflows to help you get the most out of Nia AI. Each example demonstrates real-world scenarios where Nia's intelligent search and code understanding capabilities shine.
     7→
     8→## 1. Universal Search Across All Sources
     9→
    10→<Frame>

    21→> **One query, all sources** - Search repos, docs, and web content in a single request
    22→>
    23→> **Auto-routing** - Nia intelligently determines which sources are most relevant
    24→
    25→**Key Features Demonstrated:**
    26→- Semantic + keyword hybrid search with configurable weighting
    27→- Cross-source ranking and deduplication
    28→- Source metadata and relevance scores
    29→- Real-time results from repos and documentation
    30→
    31→## 2. Package Search & Agent Building with AI SDK

    38→    allowFullScreen

     3→description: 'Ready-to-use configurations for AI coding agents'
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Overview
     7→
     8→People use Nia for various purposes - from deep technical research to code exploration and documentation analysis. To help AI coding agents understand and use Nia more effectively as an MCP server, we've created a collection of pre-configured rules and prompts.
     9→
    10→## Agent Rules Repository
    11→
    12→<Card title="Nia Rules for Agents" icon="github" href="https://github.com/nozomio-labs/nia-rules-for-agents">
    13→  Open-source configuration files that teach AI coding agents how to effectively use Nia's capabilities.
    14→</Card>
    15→
    16→### What's Included
    17→
    18→The repository provides ready-to-use configuration files for popular AI coding agents:
    19→
    20→- **Claude** (`.claude/agents/`)
    21→- **Codex** (`.codex/prompts/`)
    22→- **Cursor** (`.cursor/rules/`)
    23→- **Windsurf** (`.windsurf/rules/`)
    24→- **Roo Code** (`.roo/rules/`)
    25→- **Cline** (`.clinerules`)
    26→- **General Agents** (`general-agents/`)
    27→
    28→### Quick Setup
    29→
    30→<Steps>
    31→  <Step title="Clone or Download">
    32→    Get the configuration files from the [repository](https://github.com/nozomio-labs/nia-rules-for-agents).
    33→  </Step>
    34→
    35→  <Step title="Copy Configuration">
    36→    Copy the appropriate configuration file to your agent's directory:
    37→
    38→    ```bash
    39→    # For Claude
    40→    cp .claude/agents/nia-oracle.md ~/.claude/agents/
    41→
    42→    # For Codex
    43→    cp .codex/prompts/nia-oracle.md ~/.codex/prompts/
    44→
    45→    # For Cursor  
    46→    cp .cursor/rules/nia-oracle.mdc ~/.cursor/rules/
    47→
    48→    # For Windsurf
    49→    cp .windsurf/rules/nia-oracle.md ~/.windsurf/rules/
    50→    ```
    51→  </Step>
    52→
    53→  <Step title="Enable Nia MCP">
    54→    Ensure your agent has access to Nia's MCP tools. See the [Installation guide](/integrations/installation/mcp) for setup instructions.
    55→  </Step>
    56→
    57→  <Step title="Start Using">
    58→    Your agent now understands how to leverage Nia's research and search capabilities effectively.
    59→  </Step>
    60→</Steps>
    61→
    62→## Contributing
    63→
    64→The repository is open-source and welcomes contributions:
    65→
    66→- Add configurations for new AI agents
    67→- Improve existing rule sets
    68→- Share workflow patterns and examples
    69→
    70→Visit the [repository](https://github.com/nozomio-labs/nia-rules-for-agents) to contribute or report issues.
    71→
    72→<Info>
    73→  These configurations are maintained by the Nia community and are designed to help agents make the most of Nia's MCP server capabilities.
    74→</Info>
    75→
    76→
